blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ VII
BIEXTENSIONS OF SHEAVES OF GROUPSby A. Grothendieck
0. Introduction

The notion of biextension was introduced by D. MUMFORD [6], in the context of formal groups, to express in a convenient way the relations between the formal groups associated to an abelian scheme and the dual abelian scheme. In the present exposé, we make a systematic study of this notion in the general context of sheaves of groups on a given site, and show in particular that this notion fits in a remarkably simple way into the classical cohomological formalism of the \(\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathrm L}\) and \(\mathrm{RHom}\) (3.6.4 and 3.6.5). In the following exposé, we will make explicit the most interesting particular cases, and in particular the case of biextensions of group schemes by the multiplicative group \(G_m\) (working with sites such as the Zariski site, or the fpqc site, or some intermediate site); we will see in particular that if \(A\) and \(B\) are two abelian schemes over a scheme \(S\), the biextensions of \((A,B)\) by \(G_m\) have a remarkable interpretation as being, essentially, the classical divisorial correspondences on \(A,B\). Thus the cohomological significance of the notion of divisorial correspondence (implicit already in Tate's duality theorem for abelian varieties over p-adic fields) is made explicit in a very convenient way. This interpretation of divisorial correspondences is also convenient for "following" the fate of such a correspondence, when one makes \(A\) and \(B\) (assumed defined over the fraction field of a discrete valuation ring) "degenerate", with the help of Néron's theory: the results of VIII will show us that one finds for example a biextension of the pair of identity components of the Néron models, whence a biextension of the connected special fibers of the Néron models, playing the role of a specialization of the initial divisorial correspondence. These results will be used in IX, in conjunction with the monodromy theorem (I 3), to prove certain results on the Néron models of abelian varieties (the most important being the "semi-stable reduction theorem" for the Néron model).

In all that follows, we work (unless expressly mentioned to the contrary) in a fixed \(U\)-topos, denoted \(T\), i.e. (SGA 4 IV) \(T\) is a category equivalent to the category of sheaves on a suitable \(U\)-site (which one may take, if one wishes, to be \(T\), endowed with its canonical topology). We will often identify, in the terminology, the objects of \(T\) with the sheaves (of sets) on \(T\). The Groups, extensions of Groups etc. which will be in question will all be relative to this topos \(T\).
1. Complements on extensions of Groups
1.1. Let \(P\) and \(G\) be two Groups of \(T\), and
\[(1.1.1)\qquad 1\longrightarrow G\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{i}}E \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{j}}P\longrightarrow1\]

an extension of \(P\) by \(G\); thus \(E\) is a Group, \(j:E\longrightarrow P\) is an epimorphism of Groups, and \(i\) induces an isomorphism of \(G\) with \(\operatorname{Ker}j\). We are going to reinterpret the datum of such an extension in different terms, which will be convenient for us in the definition and the study of biextensions.
1.1.2. First of all, the homomorphism \(i\) allows us to make \(G\) operate in two ways on \(E\), on the left and on the right, by setting

for \(g,g'\in G(S)\), \(x\in E(S)\) (\(S\) an arbitrary object of \(T\)). If we consider \(E\), thanks to \(j\), as an object "above \(P\)" i.e. as an object of the induced topos \(T_{/P}\), then it amounts to the same to say that the group \(G_P=G\times P\) induced by \(G\) in \(T_{/P}\) operates on \(E\) on the left and on the right. Moreover, it is well known that each of the two operations makes \(E\) a torsor (on the left resp. on the right) over \(P\), with group \(G_P\), and that one even obtains, more precisely, a bitorsor under \((G_P,G_P)\). Let us recall [2] that this means that \(E\) is a right torsor \(E_d\) under the right action of \(G_P\), and that the left action induces an isomorphism of \(G_P\) with the Group of automorphisms of \(E_d\) (= sheaf of groups of automorphisms of \(E\) which commute with the right action of \(G_P\) on \(E\)). This condition is equivalent to the symmetric condition, namely that the left action of \(G_P\) on \(E\) makes \(E\) a left torsor \(E_g\), and that the right action induces an isomorphism of the opposite Group \(G_P^o\) of \(G_P\) with the Group of automorphisms of \(E_g\).

1.1.2.1. When \(T\) is the "punctual topos" i.e. is the category of sets (equivalent to the category of sheaves on the one-point topological space), so that \(P\) and \(G\) are ordinary groups, then the datum of a bitorsor \(E\) under \((G_P,G_P)\) is manifestly equivalent to the datum, for every \(p\in P\), of a bitorsor \(E_p\) under \((G,G)\) (namely the fiber of \(E\) at \(p\)). An analogous interpretation may be given in the general case, by the remark that the datum of a bitorsor \(E\) under \((G_P,G_P)\) is equivalent to the datum, for every "point" \(p\in P(S)\) (\(S\) an arbitrary object of \(T\)), of a bitorsor \(E_p\) under the pair of Groups \((G_S,G_S)\) over \(S\), in such a way that the formation of \(E_p\) be "compatible with the base changes for every morphism \(S'\longrightarrow S\)"; i.e. if \(p':S'\longrightarrow P\) is the composite morphism, one supposes given moreover an isomorphism of bitorsors of \(E_{p'}\) with the inverse image of \(E_p\) by \(S'\longrightarrow S\), — these isomorphisms being subject to the usual transitivity condition for the case of a composite of two morphisms \(S''\longrightarrow S'\longrightarrow S\). When one spells out this statement in canonical language (SGA 1 VI), it reduces to the following tautology: the datum of a bitorsor under the pair of Groups \((G_P,G_P)\) over \(P\) is equivalent to a cartesian section, over the category \(T_{/P}\), of the fibered category of bitorsors with group \((G_S,G_S)\) over a variable base \(S\in\operatorname{ob}T_{/P}\); more precisely still, the functor "value at \(P\)" induces an equivalence of the category of cartesian sections in question with the category of bitorsors under \((G_P,G_P)\). This statement follows of course trivially from the fact that \(P\) is a final object of the base category \(T_{/P}\) of our fibered category; hence for any fibered category over \(T_{/P}\) the functor "value at \(S\)" is an equivalence between the category of cartesian sections of the said fibered category, and the fiber category at \(P\). Nevertheless, despite its tautological nature, this interpretation will be useful to us for expressing certain compatibilities in a language close to the "set-theoretic" case, by using points with values in an object \(S\) instead of the ordinary points.

1.1.3. The bitorsor structure 1.1.2 under \((G_P,G_P)\) on \(E\), in terms of the extension structure (1.1.1), used only a part of the multiplicative structure of \(E\), namely the multiplication of pairs of "points" of which at least one comes from a point of \(G\) (via \(i\)). We are now going to interpret the multiplicative structure of \(E\) as an additional structure on the bitorsor under consideration. For this, let us first consider two sections \(p,p'\) of \(P\), whence, with the notations of 1.1.2.1, two bitorsors \(E_p,E_{p'}\) under \((G,G)\), namely the inverse images in \(E\) of the said sections \(p,p':e\longrightarrow P\) (NB \(e\) denotes the final object of \(T\)). It is trivial then that the multiplication of \(E\) induces a morphism of objects of \(T\)
\[\varphi:E_p\times E_{p'}\longrightarrow E_{pp'}\]
which satisfies, in terms of the left and right structures of the three bitorsors in play (and taking as usual points with values in an arbitrary object \(S\in\operatorname{ob}T\))
\[(1.1.3.0)\qquad \begin{cases} \varphi(gx,x')=g\,\varphi(x,x'),\quad \varphi(x,x'g')=\varphi(x,x')g',\\ \varphi(xg,x')=\varphi(x,gx'). \end{cases}\]
(\(g,g'\in G(S)\), \(x\in E_p(S)\), \(x'\in E_{p'}(S)\)). The third of these relations is interpreted, in terms of the contracted fibered product [2]
\[E_p\mathbin{\mathop{\times}^{G}}E_{p'},\]
by saying that the morphism \(\varphi\) factors as
\[E_p\times E_{p'}\longrightarrow E_p\mathbin{\mathop{\times}^{G}}E_{p'} \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\varphi_{p,p'}}}E_{pp'},\]
where the first arrow is the canonical morphism of passage to the quotient. On the other hand, the first two relations mean that the morphism
\[(1.1.3.1)\qquad \varphi_{p,p'}:E_p\mathbin{\mathop{\times}^{G}}E_{p'}\longrightarrow E_{pp'}\]
which we have just obtained is a homomorphism of bitorsors, taking into account the bitorsor structure on the first member for which the operations

on the left (resp. on the right) of \(G\) come from the operations on the left (resp. on the right) of \(G\) on the first factor \(E_p\) (resp. on the second factor \(E_{p'}\)). Let us note that (like every homomorphism of bitorsors under a given pair of Groups) the homomorphism \(\varphi_{p,p'}\) is necessarily an
<u>isomorphism</u>

The definition of the preceding isomorphism (1.1.3.1) extends at once to the case where \(p,p'\), instead of being sections of \(P\), are points of \(P\) with values in an arbitrary object \(S\) of \(T\): it suffices to apply what precedes to the induced topos \(T_{/S}\) and to the extension of Groups over \(S\) deduced from (1.1.1) by base change. One then observes that the formation of the preceding isomorphisms \(\varphi_{p,p'}\) is "compatible with every base change" \(S'\longrightarrow S\) (i.e. corresponds to a homomorphism of cartesian sections of the fibered category which one thinks of over the category \(T_{/P\times P}\) of double arrows \(p,p':S\rightrightarrows P\) with target \(P\)). The datum of the system of the \(\varphi_{p,p'}\) satisfying this condition amounts again, of course, to the datum of \(\varphi_{p,p'}\) in the "universal" case, corresponding to the case where \(S=P\times P\) and where \(p,p'\) are respectively the two projections \(\operatorname{pr}_i\) \((i=1,2)\) of \(P\times P\) into \(P\). It is then interpreted as an isomorphism of bitorsors under \((G_{P\times P},G_{P\times P})\):
\[(1.1.3.2)\qquad \varphi:\operatorname{pr}_1^*(E)\mathbin{\mathop{\times}^{G}}\operatorname{pr}_2^*(E) \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}}\pi^*(E),\]
where
\[\pi:P\times P\longrightarrow P\]

is the morphism of objects of \(T\) defining the multiplicative structure of the Group \(P\). It is however in the form of the data (1.1.3.1) that the compatibility properties which will follow are stated most conveniently, avoiding recourse to diagrams less close to intuition.

1.1.4. In writing the relations (1.1.3.0), which allowed us to express the composition law given on \(E\) in terms of isomorphisms of bitorsors (1.1.3.1), we used the associativity of this composition law only in the case of the product of three "points" of \(E\) at least one of which comes from a point of \(G\). If one wants to express the full associativity in terms of the \(\varphi_{p,p'}\), one is led to write, for three points \(p,p',p''\) of \(P\) (with values in an object \(S\) of \(T\)), that the following diagram is commutative:

\[(1.1.4.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3.5em@R=2.5em{ & E_{pp'}E_{p''}\ar[dr]^{\varphi_{pp',p''}} & \\ E_pE_{p'}E_{p''}\ar[ur]^{\varphi_{p,p'}\wedge\mathrm{id}}\ar[dr]_{\mathrm{id}\wedge\varphi_{p',p''}} && E_{pp'p''}\\ & E_pE_{p'p''}\ar[ur]_{\varphi_{p,p'p''}} & }\]

In this diagram, we have written the contracted product of bitorsors without \(\mathbin{\mathop{\times}^{G_S}}\) signs, it being understood that the contracted multiplication of two bitorsors \(Q,R\) is carried out using (for the passage to the quotient in \(Q\times R\)) the operation of the structural group \(G\) on the right on \(Q\) and on the left on \(R\). We know moreover that this contracted product operation is associative up to a canonical isomorphism [2], and we make the identification \((E_pE_{p'})E_{p''}\simeq E_p(E_{p'}E_{p''})\), by simply writing the two members as \(E_pE_{p'}E_{p''}\). The obvious conventions of this nature will be used in the rest of this exposé, without being expressly pointed out each time.

1.1.4.2. Just as the datum of the isomorphisms \(\varphi_{p,p'}\) (1.1.3.1) reduces to the "universal" case \(S=P\times P\), \(p=\operatorname{pr}_1,p'=\operatorname{pr}_2\) considered in (1.1.3.2), likewise for the verification of the associativity conditions (1.1.4.1), one may again restrict oneself to the "universal" case where \(S=P\times P\times P\), \(p=\operatorname{pr}_1,p'=\operatorname{pr}_2,p''=\operatorname{pr}_3\). We leave to the reader the task of writing the diagram in question with the notations of his choice.
1.1.5. Conversely, the Groups \(P\) and \(G\) of \(T\) being assumed given, suppose given in addition a bitorsor
\[(1.1.5.1)\qquad j:E\longrightarrow P\]

under \((G_P,G_P)\) (NB the two actions of \(G_P\) on \(E\), or what amounts to the same, of \(G\) on \(E\), are understood in the notations), and on the other hand a system of isomorphisms of bitorsors over a variable base \(S\)
\[(1.1.5.2)\qquad \varphi_{p,p'}:E_pE_{p'}\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}}E_{pp'} \qquad(p,p'\in P(S)),\]

compatible with base change \(S'\longrightarrow S\); in other words, an isomorphism of bitorsors (1.1.3.2). Suppose that the corresponding diagrams (1.1.4.1) are commutative. I claim that there exists on \(E\) a unique structure of extension of \(P\) by \(G\), such that the preceding data are deduced from this structure by the constructions of 1.1.2 and 1.1.3.
Let us note first that when one takes in (1.1.5.2) for \(p,p'\) the unit section (1) of \(P\), one finds an isomorphism
\[(1.1.5.3)\qquad \varphi_{1,1}:E_1\mathrel{\mathop{\longleftarrow}^{\sim}}E_1 \mathbin{\mathop{\times}^{G}}E_1,\]
whence, taking the contracted product (on the left, say) of the two members with the inverse bitorsor \(E_1^{-1}\) of \(E_1\) [2], a canonical isomorphism
\[(1.1.5.4)\qquad E_1\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}} \text{unit bitorsor }{}_sG_d\]
(\(=G\) operating on itself by left and right translations), taking into account the canonical isomorphism
\[E_1^{-1}E_1\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}}\text{unit bitorsor }{}_sG_d\]
of loc. cit. (valid for every bitorsor). It amounts to the same to say that we have a section \(1_E\) of \(E_1\) hence of \(E\):
\[(1.1.5.5)\qquad 1_E\in\Gamma(E_1)=\operatorname{Hom}(e,E_1)\subset\Gamma(E)\]
(namely the image under (1.1.5.4) of the marked section of \({}_sG_d\)), satisfying the relation
On the other hand, the datum of a morphism of torsors \(\varphi\) (1.1.3.2) is manifestly equivalent to the datum of a morphism
\[(1.1.5.7)\qquad \pi_E:E\times E\longrightarrow E,\qquad\text{also denoted }(x,y)\longmapsto xy\]
satisfying the conditions
\[(1.1.5.8)\qquad j(xy)=j(x)j(y)\qquad(x,y\in E(S))\]

(i.e. \(j\) is compatible with the multiplicative structures \(\pi_E\) resp. \(\pi\) on \(E\) resp. \(P\)), and the three relations (1.1.3.0) (beginning preferably with the last of these). Finally, one defines a morphism of objects of \(T\)
\[i:G\longrightarrow E\]
by the formula
\[(1.1.5.9)\qquad i(g)=g\cdot1_E=1_E\cdot g\]

(the identity of the last two members in this formula having already been noted with the construction of \(1_E\)). This being settled, I claim that the multiplication law \(\pi_E\) on \(E\) makes \(E\) a Group, admitting \(1_E\) as unit section, and that \(j:E\longrightarrow P\) and \(i:G\longrightarrow E\) are homomorphisms of Groups which make the Group \(E\) an extension of \(P\) by \(G\).

Indeed, the associativity of the composition law of \(E\) is none other than the commutativity of the diagrams (1.1.4.1). The construction of \(\pi_E\) via the isomorphisms \(\varphi_{p,p'}\) (1.1.3.1) shows on the other hand that for every \(x\in E(S)\), the left and right translations by \(x\) in \(E(S)\) are injective. Since \(1_E1_E=1_E\) by (1.1.5.6), we conclude at once that \(1_E\) is a two-sided unit of \(E\). We leave to the reader the verification of the existence of an inverse of an \(x\in E(S)\) over \(p\in P(S)\), using a canonical isomorphism deduced from \(\varphi_{p,p^{-1}}\):
\[(1.1.5.10)\qquad E_{p^{-1}}\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}}(E_p)^o.\]
Hence \(E\) is indeed a Group. We have already pointed out that \(j\) is compatible with the composition laws (1.1.5.8), and the same holds for (i), for we will have, for \(g,g'\in G(S)\):

\[\begin{aligned} i(g)i(g')&=(g\cdot1_E)(g'\cdot1_E)=(g\cdot1_E\cdot g')\cdot1_E &&\text{(last relation of (1.1.3.0))}\\ &=g\cdot1_E\cdot g' &&\text{(since (1_E) is a unit)}\\ &=g\cdot(1_E\cdot g')=g\cdot(g'\cdot1_E)\\ &=(gg')\cdot1_E=i(gg'). \end{aligned}\]

Since \(j\) is the structural projection of a torsor, it is an epimorphism of objects of \(T\); it remains to see that (i) induces an isomorphism of \(G\) with \(\operatorname{Ker}j=E_1\), which is clear by the definition (1.1.5.9) and the construction of \(1_E\).

1.1.6. We will grant that the preceding arguments prove that for a fixed topos \(T\), the preceding constructions furnish an equivalence between the category of extensions of Groups of \(T\) (1.1.1) (where \(P\) and \(G\) as well as \(E\) are supposed variable), and the category of systems \((P,G,E,\varphi)\), where \(P\) and \(G\) are Groups of \(T\), \(E\) a bitorsor under \((G_P,G_P)\) on \(P\), and finally \(\varphi\) an isomorphism of bitorsors (1.1.3.2) (or if one prefers, a system of isomorphisms of bitorsors (1.1.3.1) compatible with base changes), satisfying the associativity condition expressed by the commutativity of the diagram (1.1.4.2) (or if one prefers, of the diagrams (1.1.4.1)).

We leave to the reader the task of carrying out the obvious explicitation, in terms of the preceding dictionary, of the notion of inverse (resp. direct) image of an extension (1.1.1) by a morphism of Groups
\[P'\longrightarrow P\qquad(\text{resp. }G\longrightarrow G'),\]

or by these two operations simultaneously. We likewise leave to him the task of making explicit a compatibility of the preceding equivalence of categories with the inverse image functor \(f^*\) by a morphism of topoi
\[f:T'\longrightarrow T\ ;\]

for this, one should use the datum \(\varphi\) in the form (1.1.3.2) (in preference to the \(\varphi_{p,p'}\) of (1.1.3.1)), which then defines a corresponding datum \(f^*(\varphi)\) on the topos \(T'\).

1.2. Commutative case. In all that follows from 1.4 on, we will only be interested in commutative extensions of Groups. We will therefore suppose \(P\) and \(G\) commutative, and we propose here to make explicit, in terms of the description \((P,G,E,\varphi)\) of an extension in terms of bitorsors, the fact that \(E\) is a commutative Group. A first obviously necessary condition (and which in fact expresses that \(i(G)\) be a central sub-Group of \(E\)) is that the bitorsors \(E_p\) \((p\in P(S))\) be ordinary torsors, i.e. that the left and right operations of \(G\) on \(E_p\) coincide. It obviously amounts to the same to say that \(E\) on \(P\) is an ordinary torsor under \(G_P\). (It will therefore be unnecessary from now on to speak of bitorsors, and we will consider only ordinary torsors.) This preliminary condition being supposed fulfilled, the commutativity condition of \(E\) is then expressed by the commutativity of the following diagrams (where \(p,p'\in P(S)\)):
\[(1.2.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=4em@R=3em{ E_pE_{p'}\ar[r]^{\varphi_{p,p'}}\ar[d]_{\mathrm{sym}} & E_{pp'}\ar[d]^{\mathrm{id}}\\ E_{p'}E_p\ar[r]_{\varphi_{p',p}} & E_{p'p} }\ ,\]

where the first vertical arrow is the canonical commutativity isomorphism (defined thanks to the fact that \(G\) is now commutative; we have moreover \(pp'=p'p\) since \(P\) is commutative). This condition is obviously equivalent to the corresponding condition in the universal situation \(S=P\times P\), \(p=\operatorname{pr}_1\), \(p'=\operatorname{pr}_2\), which we refrain from rewriting here.
1.3. Relations with rigidified or birigidified torsors

1.3.1.0. Let \(P\) be an object of \(T\) endowed with a section \(e_P\), \(G\) a Group of \(T\), \(E\) a torsor under \(G_P\). We call a rigidification of \(E\) (relative to the given section \(e_P\) of \(P\)) a trivialization of the torsor \(E_1=e_P^*(E)\) under \(G\), i.e. a section of this torsor. The terminology adopted is inspired by the case where \(G\) is commutative and where we have
\[(1.3.1.1)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_{p_T}(P,G)\simeq(e),\]

where the first member denotes the set of morphisms transforming \(e_P\) into the unit section \(e_G\) of \(G\), — a relation which is equivalent to the fact that the natural homomorphism \(\Gamma(G)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(P,G)\), associating to a section \(g\) of \(G\) the constant morphism \(P\longrightarrow G\) with value \(g\), is an isomorphism:
\[(1.3.1.2)\qquad \Gamma(G)\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}}\operatorname{Hom}(P,G).\]

Indeed one sees immediately that the preceding equivalent relations are also equivalent to saying that every automorphism of the torsor \(E\) which respects the given rigidification is the identity automorphism (since the automorphisms in question correspond precisely to the elements of the first member of (1.3.1.1)); in particular, granting the condition (1.3.1.1), the category of torsors under \(G_P\) rigidified relative to \(e_P\) is « rigid ».

1.3.2. Let moreover \(Q\) be an object of \(T\) endowed with a section \(e_Q\), and let us now denote by \(E\) a torsor under \(G_{P\times Q}\). As \(e_Q\) defines a section \(e_{P\times Q/P}\) of \(P\times Q\) over \(P\), one can consider on \(E\) the rigidifications \(\alpha\) relative to this section (placing oneself in the induced topos \(T_{/P}\)), which are spelled out as the sections of the torsor induced by \(E\) on \(P\times e_Q\). Exchanging the roles of \(P\) and \(Q\), one can consider the rigidifications \(\beta\) on \(E\) relative to the section \(e_{P\times Q/Q}\) of \(P\times Q\) over \(Q\) defined by \(e_P\). Two such partial rigidifications are said to be compatible if the sections \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) coincide on \(e_P\times e_Q\), i.e. define one and the same section of the torsor \(E_{1,1}\) under \(G\), the inverse image of \(E\) by the section \((e_P,e_Q)\) of \(P\times Q\). We also call a birigidification of the torsor \(E\) relative to the pair of sections \((e_P,e_Q)\), a pair of two compatible partial rigidifications. If \(G\) is commutative, one sees at once that for the torsor \(E\) to admit a birigidification \((\alpha,\beta)\), it is necessary and sufficient that it admit partial rigidifications \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\), and one can then find a birigidification in which \(\alpha\) (or \(\beta\)) is chosen in advance; indeed, it then suffices to correct \(\beta\) by the morphism \(Q\longrightarrow G\) composed as \(Q\longrightarrow e\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\lambda}}G\), where the second arrow \(\lambda\) is defined by the property that \(\beta e_Q=(\alpha e_P)\lambda\). Still in the commutative case, let us note that if \(E,E'\) are two birigidified torsors under \(G_{P\times Q}\), it amounts to the same to say that they are isomorphic as plain \(G_{P\times Q}\)-torsors, or as birigidified \(G_{P\times Q}\)-torsors; in other words, if there exists an isomorphism \(u:E\longrightarrow E'\) of \(G_{P\times Q}\)-torsors, one can find an isomorphism \(v:E\longrightarrow E'\) of \(G_{P\times Q}\)-torsors which respects the birigidifications. Indeed, considering the discrepancy of \(u\) for the given birigidifications, one finds morphisms \(f:P\longrightarrow G\) and \(g:Q\longrightarrow G\) such that \(fe_P=ge_Q\), i.e. \(a\in G(e)\); there exists then a morphism \(P\times Q\longrightarrow G\) whose composites with \(e_{P\times Q/P}\) and \(e_{P\times Q/Q}\) are respectively \(f\) and \(g\) \(savoir (h(p,q)=f(p)g(q)a^{-1})\), and one can take \(v=uh\).

1.3.3. The terminology of 1.3.1 extends at once to the case where we have a bitorsor \(E\) under \((G_P,G_P)\), it being understood that a rigidification of the bitorsor \(E\) must be an isomorphism of \(e_P(E)\) with the trivial bitorsor \({}_sG_d\), i.e. is defined by a section \(e_E\) of \(e_P(E)\) which is central, i.e. which satisfies the condition \(g.e_E=e_E.g\) \((g\in G(S))\). One likewise extends the terminology of birigidifications to the case of bitorsors.

1.3.4. Let us return to the case where we are given two Groups \(P\) and \(G\), and let us choose the unit section \(e_P\) to define rigidifications of bitorsors on \(P\), and birigidifications of bitorsors on \(P\times P\). We have natural functors
\[(1.3.4.1)\qquad \mathbf{EXT}(P;G)\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{i}} \mathbf{BITORSRIG}(P,G)\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\delta}} \mathbf{BITORSBIRIG}(P,P;G),\]

where the three expressions written denote respectively the category of extensions of Groups of \(P\) by \(G\), the category of bitorsors under \((G_P,G_P)\) rigidified for \(e_P\), and the category of bitorsors under \((G_{P\times P},G_{P\times P})\) birigidified for \((e_P,e_P)\). One defines the functor (i) by associating to the extension \(E\) of \(P\) by \(G\) the corresponding bitorsor on \(P\) (1.1.6), rigidified by the unit section of \(E\). One defines the functor \(\delta\) by the formula
\[(1.3.4.2)\qquad \delta(E)=\pi^*(E)\operatorname{pr}_1^*(E)^{-1}\operatorname{pr}_2^*(E)^{-1},\]

where \(\pi:P\times P\longrightarrow P\) is the composition law of \(P\), \(\operatorname{pr}_1\) and \(\operatorname{pr}_2\) are the two projections. We have a canonical birigidification on \(\delta(E)\). Indeed, the restriction of \(\delta(E)\) to \(P\times e_P\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\operatorname{id}_P^*(E)\operatorname{id}_P^*(E)^{-1}e^*(E)^{-1}\), where \(e=\operatorname{pr}_2\operatorname{inj}_1\) is the constant morphism \(P\longrightarrow P\) with value \(e_P\), so that thanks to the given rigidification of \(E\), we have an isomorphism \(e^*(E)\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}}\) trivial bitorsor, whence an isomorphism of the restriction of \(\delta(E)\) to \(P\times e_P\) with the trivial bitorsor. One defines in a symmetric way a partial rigidification relative to \(e_P\times P\), and one verifies trivially that these two partial rigidifications are compatible.

1.3.4.3. It will be observed that the composite \(\delta i\) of the functors \(\delta\) and \(i\) of (1.3.4.1) is canonically isomorphic to the trivial functor, i.e. to the constant functor with value the trivial birigidified bitorsor on \(P\times P\). Indeed, for every extension \(E\) of \(P\) by \(G\), the isomorphism \(\varphi\) of (1.1.3.2) evidently defines a trivialization of \(\delta i(E)\) compatible with its canonical birigidification.

blocks.title: Proposition 1.3.5.
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.3.5. Let \(P\) and \(Q\) be two Groups of \(T\).

a) Suppose that every morphism of sheaves of sets from \(P\times P\) to \(G\) which is trivial on \(P\times e_P\) and on \(e_P\times P\) is trivial. Then the functor \(i\) of (1.3.4.1) is fully faithful. In particular, on a bitorsor \(E\) under \((G_P,G_P)\), birigidified relative to \(e_P\), i.e. endowed with a central section \(e_E\) of \(E_1=e_P^*(E)\), there is at most one structure of extension of \(P\) by \(G\), compatible with its bitorsor structure and admitting \(e_E\) as unit section.

b) Suppose moreover that every morphism of sheaves of sets from \(P\times P\times P\) to \(G\) which is trivial on the partial products \(P\times P\times e_P\), \(P\times e_P\times P\) and \(e_P\times P\times P\) is trivial. Let \(E\) be a torsor under \(G_P\). In order that there exist on \(E\) a structure of extension of \(P\) by \(G\) compatible with its structure of bitorsor, it is necessary and sufficient that the bitorsor under \((G_{P\times P},G_{P\times P})\) underlying \(\delta(E)=\pi^*(E)\operatorname{pr}_1^*(E)^{-1}\operatorname{pr}_2^*(E)^{-1}\) be trivial. When this is so, for every central section \(e_E\) of \(E_1=e_P^*(E)\), i.e. every rigidification of \(E\) relative to \(e_P\), there exists a unique extension structure on \(E\), compatible with its bitorsor structure and admitting \(e_E\) as unit section, i.e. \(E\), considered as a bitorsor rigidified relative to \(e_P\), is in the essential image of \(i\). On the other hand, there exists one and only one trivialization of the birigidified bitorsor \(\delta(E)\).

In terms of the diagram (1.3.4.1), we may therefore say that the essential image of the fully faithful functor \(i\) is "the kernel" of the functor \(\delta\), or again, figuratively speaking, that the diagram (1.3.4.1) is an "exact sequence of pointed categories", – and even of categories with pseudo-group structures (in the terminology introduced in 2.5.1 below).

Let us prove 1.3.5. a) Let \(E,E'\) be two extensions of \(P\) by \(G\), \(f:E\longrightarrow E'\) a morphism of the underlying rigidified bitorsors; let us show that \(f\) is a morphism of extensions, or what manifestly amounts to the same, a morphism of Groups. We must prove that we have \(f(xy)=f(x)f(y)\); now (denoting by \(j':E'\longrightarrow P\) the structural morphism of the torsor \(E'\) over \(P\)) we will have \(j'f(xy)=j'(f(x)f(y))\), for the two members are respectively equal to \(j(f(xy))\) and to \(j(f(x)f(y))\), which are equal since \(j\) is multiplicative. We therefore have
where
\[u:P\times P\longrightarrow G\]
is a well-determined morphism. Setting \(x=1\) then \(y=1\) in the preceding relation, we find that we have (taking into account \(f(1)=1\))
i.e. \(u\) is trivial on \(P\times e_P\) and on \(e_P\times P\), whence it follows by hypothesis that \(u\) is trivial, hence \(f(xy)=f(x)f(y)\), qed.

b) The necessity of the condition envisaged on \(E\) in order that it admit an extension structure compatible with its bitorsor structure being trivial (1.3.4.3), it remains to prove its sufficiency in order to prove the first assertion of b). Now, using a trivialization \(\varphi\) of \(\delta(E)\), we deduce from it, by inverse image by the unit section of \(P\times P\), a trivialization of \(E_1=e_P^*(E)\) (compare 1.5.1), i.e. a rigidification of \(E\) relative to \(e_P\). Let us suppose it fixed. A given trivialization \(\varphi\) of \(\delta(E)\) is not necessarily compatible with this birigidification; its restrictions to \(P\times e_P\) and to \(e_P\times P\) are expressed, in terms of the natural partial rigidifications of \(\delta(E)\), by central morphisms
\[f:P\longrightarrow G,\quad g:P\longrightarrow G,\]
having the same restrictions to the unit section of \(P\): \(f(1)=g(1)=a\in\Gamma G\). Then the central morphism
\[h:P\times P\longrightarrow G,\quad h(p,q)=f(p)g(q)a^{-1}\]

has as restrictions to \(P\times e_P\) and \(e_P\times P\) the morphisms \(f\) and \(g\). Consequently, correcting \(\varphi\) by \(h\) (i.e. replacing it by \(\varphi(p,q)h(p,q)^{-1}\)), we find a trivialization of \(\delta(E)\) compatible with its birigidification. It will moreover be noted that such a trivialization is determined modulo a central morphism \(P\times P\longrightarrow G\) which is trivial on \(P\times e_P\) and on \(e_P\times P\), hence which is trivial, hence this \(\varphi\) is unique, which is the last assertion of 1.3.5 b). The proof will be finished if we prove that this \(\varphi\), considered as an isomorphism of the form (1.1.3.2), satisfies the associativity condition (1.1.4.1), taking into account that we already know by the choice of \(\varphi\) that \(e_E\) is a two-sided unit for the composition law defined by \(\varphi\). Now, the two composites in (1.1.4.1) are isomorphisms of bitorsors under \((G_{P\times P\times P},G_{P\times P\times P})\), hence differ by a central morphism
\[u:P\times P\times P\longrightarrow G,\]
thanks to the formula
\[(xy)z=u(jx,jy,jz)\;x(yz).\]

Setting in this formula successively \(y=z=1\), then \(z=x=1\), then \(x=y=1\), and using the fact that 1 is a two-sided unit for the composition law envisaged, we find that \(u\) is trivial on the three pieces \(P\times e_P\times e_P\), \(e_P\times P\times e_P\), \(e_P\times e_P\times P\), which implies by hypothesis that it is trivial, hence that \((xy)z=x(yz)\), which completes the proof.

blocks.title: Corollary 1.3.6.
blocks.html: Corollary 1.3.6. Suppose that every morphism of sheaves of sets \(P\times G\longrightarrow G\) which is trivial on \(P\times e_G\) and on \(e_P\times G\) is trivial. Then, if \(G\) is commutative, \(G\) is central in every extension of Groups of \(P\) by \(G\). If \(P\) is also commutative, and if the hypothesis of 1.3.5 a) is satisfied, then every extension of Groups \(E\) of \(P\) by \(G\) is commutative.

For the first assertion, we must prove that the operation of \(P\) on \(G\) defined by the extension \(G\) is trivial. Now this operation is expressed by a morphism \(u:P\times G\longrightarrow G\), such that the restrictions to \(P\times e_G\) and to \(e_P\times G\) of the corresponding morphism \((p,g)\longmapsto u(p,g)g^{-1}\) are obviously trivial, hence this last morphism is trivial i.e. we have \(u(p,g)=g\), which is what we wanted to establish. Let us now note that if \(E\) is an extension of \(P\) by \(G\), then the Group \(E^{\mathrm o}\) opposite to \(E\), which is an extension of \(P^{\mathrm o}\) by \(G^{\mathrm o}\), has as underlying bitorsor structure \(i(E^{\mathrm o})\) the one deduced from the bitorsor \(i(E)\) by exchanging in it the left and right operations. When \(E\) is a central extension, we therefore have \(i(E)=i(E^{\mathrm o})\), and if moreover \(P\) is commutative i.e. \(P=P^{\mathrm o}\), we find by the uniqueness assertion 1.3.5 a) that \(E=E^{\mathrm o}\) i.e. that \(E\) is commutative. That establishes the corollary.
1.3.7. An interesting particular case where all the hypotheses envisaged in 1.3.5 and 1.3.6 are satisfied is that where we have
\[(1.3.7.1)\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathrm{pt}}(P,G)=e\quad\text{(final sheaf)},\]

where the first member denotes the sheaf of morphisms of pointed sheaves of sets from \(P\) to \(G\). This condition can also be written, independently of the marked sections, in the form \(analogue à (1.3.1.2)\)
\[(1.3.7.2)\qquad G\xrightarrow{\sim}\underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{\mathrm{ens}}(P,G),\]

where the second member denotes the sheaf of morphisms for the sheaves of sets underlying \(P\) and \(G\). These conditions also mean that for every object \(Q\) of \(T\), the map \(u\longmapsto u\circ\operatorname{pr}_2\)
\[(1.3.7.3)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{ens}}(Q,G)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{ens}}(P\times Q,G)\]

is bijective. One sees that this property of \(P\) (for fixed \(G\)) is stable under cartesian products, hence in particular that it implies the same property for the cartesian products \(P\times P\), \(P\times P\times P\) etc. It is immediate from then on that it implies the hypotheses made in 1.3.5 and in 1.3.6; moreover, it implies that the categories which intervene in (1.3.4.1) are all rigid, i.e. that every automorphism of an object of one of these categories is the identity. Moreover, as the condition (1.3.7.1) on \(P,G\) is stable under every base change \(S\longrightarrow e\), it follows at once by descent, from the uniqueness result 1.3.5 a), that in order that a rigidified bitorsor \(E\) on \(P\) correspond indeed to an extension of \(P\) by \(G\), it suffices that this be so locally on \(e\), which (by virtue of 1.3.5 b)) is also expressed by the vanishing of the section
\[\delta(E)\in\Gamma\,\mathrm R^1h_*(G_{P\times P})\]
defined by \(\delta(E)\), where
\[h:P\times P\longrightarrow e\]
is the structural morphism.
1.3.8. Let \(p:P\longrightarrow S\) be a morphism of schemes, such that \(p\) is coherent (i.e. quasi-compact and quasi-separated) and flat, and such that we have
\[(1.3.8.1)\qquad p_*(\mathcal O_P)\xleftarrow{\sim}\mathcal O_S.\]

Using the fact that (thanks to the coherence of \(p\)) the formation of \(p_*(\mathcal O_P)\) commutes with every flat base change, one observes at once that the conditions envisaged on the relative scheme \(P/S\) are stable under cartesian products, and are satisfied in particular for the cartesian powers \(P\times P\), \(P\times P\times P\), .... They are likewise stable under every flat base change.

If now \(G\) is an affine scheme over \(S\), it follows at once from (1.3.8.1) that the relation (1.3.7.2) is satisfied, since we have \[\operatorname{Hom}_S(P,G)=\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{alg}}(A,p_*(\mathcal O_P)) \xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{alg}}(A,\mathcal O_S),\] if \(A\) is the quasi-coherent Algebra over \(S\) such that \(G=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\). By what we have just said, the same conclusion applies to the products \(P\times P\), \(P\times P\times P\), and likewise, if \(G\) is flat over \(S\), the same conclusion applies to the relative scheme \(P_G\) over \(G\) with respect to the relative scheme \(G_G\) over \(G\).

When \(P\) and \(G\) are group schemes over \(S\), and one works with a topology on \((\mathrm{Sch})_{/S}\) coarser than the canonical topology, which allows us to identify \(P\) and \(G\) with sheaves on the preceding site, one sees that every morphism of \(P\), \(P\times P\), \(P\times P\times P\) ... into \(G\), compatible with the marked sections, is trivial; likewise if \(G\) is flat, every \(S\)-morphism \(P\times G\longrightarrow G\) compatible with the marked sections is trivial, as one sees by interpreting it as a \(G\)-morphism from \(P_G\) into \(G_G\). This shows therefore that we are under the conditions of application of 1.3.5, and also of 1.3.6 when \(G\) is flat. Moreover, the three categories intervening in (1.3.4.1) are rigid.

1.3.8.2. The most interesting case where the condition (1.3.8.1) is satisfied, and remains satisfied after every extension of the base, is that where \(P\) is an abelian scheme over \(S\); we are then even under the conditions of 1.3.7. In this case, the conclusions of 3.5 and of 3.6 are therefore valid for every Group \(G\) affine over \(S\). This is the case initially envisaged by J.P. SERRE, by which the preceding developments are visibly inspired. An interesting case where (1.3.8.1) is satisfied without being so universally is that where \(P\) is the Néron model of an abelian scheme over the fraction field of a discrete valuation ring with spectrum \(S\).
1.4. Extensions of a free commutative Group
Let \(I\) be an object of \(T\), and let us take
\[(1.4.1)\qquad P=\mathbb Z[I],\]

the free \(\mathbb Z\)-Module generated by \(I\) (SGA 4 IV 11). To every extension \(E\) of \(P\) by a given \(\mathbb Z\)-Module \(G\) (NB from now on only commutative extensions of groups will be in question), let us associate the corresponding torsor under \(G_P\), then its restriction to \(I\) by the canonical morphism
\[I\longrightarrow P=\mathbb Z[I].\]
We thus find a canonical functor
\[(1.4.2)\qquad \operatorname{EXT}(P,G)\longrightarrow\operatorname{TORS}(I,G_I).\]
I claim that this functor is an equivalence of categories. It obviously amounts to the same to say that for every \(G\), the maps
\[(1.4.3)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathbb Z_T}(P,G)\longrightarrow H^0(I,G_I)=G(I)\] \[(1.4.4)\qquad \operatorname{Ext}^1_{\mathbb Z_T}(P,G)\longrightarrow H^1(I,G_I)\]

are isomorphisms. In fact, more generally, when \(A\) is a Ring (not necessarily commutative) of \(T\) and \(P=A[I]\) the free \(A\)-Module generated by \(I\), one finds canonical isomorphisms functorial in the variable \(A\)-Module \(G\)
\[(1.4.5)\qquad \operatorname{Ext}^i_A(A[I],G)\xrightarrow{\sim}H^i(I,G_I),\]

which for \(A=\mathbb Z\) and \(i=0,1\) coincide with (1.4.3) and (1.4.4). To define the isomorphisms (1.4.5), one recalls that the first member is the derived functor of the functor \(G\longmapsto\operatorname{Hom}_A(A[I],G)\), which by definition of \(A[I]\) is isomorphic to the functor \(G\longmapsto G(I)=H^0(I,G_I)\). As \(G\longmapsto G_I\) transforms injective Modules into injective Modules of \(T_{/I}\) (SGA 4 V), one deduces from this the desired conclusion. We leave to the reader the task of verifying the announced compatibility with the homomorphisms (1.4.3) and (1.4.4).
It will be noted that the functor 1.4.2 is compatible with inverse images by a morphism of topoi
\[f:T'\longrightarrow T.\]

pages.title: 2. The notion of biextension of abelian sheaves

blocks.html: 2. The notion of biextension of abelian sheaves
2.0. In all the rest of the present section, \(P,Q,G\) denote commutative Groups of the topos \(T\). We will frequently have to use the cartesian diagram
\[(2.0.1)\qquad \xymatrix{P\ar[d]&P\times Q\ar[l]\ar[d]\\e&Q\ar[l]}\]

and the Groups \(G_P\) (resp. \(G_Q\) resp. \(G_{P\times Q}\)) on \(P\) (resp. \(Q\), resp. \(P\times Q\)) inverse images of the Group \(G\) on \(e\). We will identify \(G_{P\times Q}\) indifferently with the inverse image \((G_P)_{P\times Q}\) of \(G_P\) by \(\operatorname{pr}_1\) or \((G_Q)_{P\times Q}\) of \(G_Q\) by \(\operatorname{pr}_2\). In these notations, the Group structures of \(P,Q,P\times Q\) do not intervene. We will moreover practically never have to use the product group structure on \(P\times Q\). On the other hand, it will be useful for us to consider on \(P\times Q\) the structure of \(P\)-Group inverse image of that of \(Q\) on \(e\),
\[(2.0.2)\qquad P\times Q=Q_P,\]
and likewise the structure of \(Q\)-Group inverse image of that of \(P\) on \(e\),
\[(2.0.3)\qquad P\times Q=P_Q.\]

Thus, on \(P\) we will have to consider the two Groups \(G_P\) and \(Q_P\), and on \(Q\) the two Groups \(G_Q\) and \(P_Q\). If \(E\) is a torsor over \(P\times Q\) with Group \(G_{P\times Q}\), it may be envisaged indifferently as a torsor over \(Q_P\) with group \(G_{P\times Q}=(G_P)_{Q_P}\), or as a torsor over \(P_Q\) with group \(G_{P\times Q}=(G_Q)_{P_Q}\). It therefore makes sense to consider on \(E\) a structure of (commutative) extension of \(Q_P\) by \(G_P\) compatible with its torsor structure, or (symmetrically) a structure of extension of \(P_Q\) by \(G_Q\) compatible with its structure of torsor. Moreover, 1.6, applied to the induced topoi \(T_{/P}\) resp. \(T_{/Q}\), furnishes us a convenient way of making such structures explicit. Using the canonical isomorphisms \(T_{/P\times Q}\xrightarrow{\sim}(T_{/P})_{/P\times Q}\) and \(T_{/P\times Q}\xrightarrow{\sim}(T_{/Q})_{/P\times Q}\), one finds the following descriptions. A structure of extension of \(Q_P\) by \(G_P\) on the torsor \(E\) is equivalent to the datum, for every object \(S\) of \(T\) and elements \(p,p'\in P(S)\), \(q\in Q(S)\), of an isomorphism of torsors under \(G_S\)
\[(2.0.4)\qquad \varphi_{p,p';q}:E_{p,q}\,E_{p',q}\xrightarrow{\sim}E_{pp',q},\]

satisfying the conditions which we are going to recall; as usual (cf. 1.1.2.1), for every point \((p,q)\in(P\times Q)(S)=P(S)\times Q(S)\), one denotes by \(E_{p,q}\) the torsor under \(G_S\) inverse image of the torsor \(E\) under \(G_{P\times Q}\) by \((p,q):S\longrightarrow P\times Q\). The conditions to impose on the data (2.0.4) are the functoriality in \(S\) (i.e. the compatibility with inverse images by morphisms \(S'\longrightarrow S\) in \(T\)), the associativity which is expressed by the commutativity of the diagrams of the form (1.1.4.1)

\[(2.0.5)\qquad \xymatrix@C=4.5em@R=3em{ &E_{pp',q}E_{p'',q}\ar[dr]^{\varphi_{pp',p'';q}}&\\ E_{p,q}E_{p',q}E_{p'',q}\ar[ur]^{\varphi_{p,p';q}\,\mathrm{id}}\ar[dr]_{\mathrm{id}\,\varphi_{p',p'';q}}&&E_{pp'p'',q}\\ &E_{p,q}E_{p'p'',q}\ar[ur]_{\varphi_{p,p'p'';q}}& }\]
(where \(p,p',p''\in P(S)\), \(q\in Q(S)\)), and finally the commutativity which is expressed by the commutativity of the diagrams of the form (1.2.1)
\[(2.0.6)\qquad \xymatrix@C=5em@R=3em{ E_{p,q}E_{p',q}\ar[r]^{\varphi_{p,p';q}}\ar[d]_{\mathrm{sym}}&E_{pp',q}\ar[d]^{\mathrm{id}}\\ E_{p',q}E_{p,q}\ar[r]_{\varphi_{p',p;q}}&E_{p'p,q} }\]
(where \(p,p'\in P(S)\), \(q\in Q(S)\)). One likewise makes explicit a structure of extension of \(P_Q\) by \(G_Q\) on \(E\) with the help of isomorphisms of torsors under \(G_S\)
\[(2.0.7)\qquad \psi_{p;q,q'}:E_{p,q}E_{p,q'}\xrightarrow{\sim}E_{p,qq'},\]

for \(p\in P(S)\) and \(q,q'\in Q(S)\), the \(\psi\) being functorial in \(S\), and satisfying the associativity and commutativity conditions symmetric to (2.0.5) and (2.0.6), expressed by the commutativity of the diagrams

\[(2.0.8)\qquad \xymatrix@C=4.5em@R=3em{ &E_{p,qq'}E_{p,q''}\ar[dr]^{\psi_{p;qq',q''}}&\\ E_{p,q}E_{p,q'}E_{p,q''}\ar[ur]^{\psi_{p;q,q'}\,\mathrm{id}}\ar[dr]_{\mathrm{id}\,\psi_{p;q',q''}}&&E_{p,qq'q''}\\ &E_{p,q}E_{p,q'q''}\ar[ur]_{\psi_{p;q,q'q''}}& }\]
\[(2.0.9)\qquad \xymatrix@C=5em@R=3em{ E_{p,q}E_{p,q'}\ar[r]^{\psi_{p;q,q'}}\ar[d]_{\mathrm{sym}}&E_{p,qq'}\ar[d]^{\mathrm{id}}\\ E_{p,q'}E_{p,q}\ar[r]_{\psi_{p;q',q}}&E_{p,q'q} }\]

blocks.title: Definition 2.1.
blocks.html:
Definition 2.1.

Let \(P,Q,G\) be abelian Groups of \(T\), \(E\) a torsor under \(G_{P\times Q}\), endowed moreover with a structure of extension of \(Q_P\) by \(G_P\) defined by isomorphisms (2.0.4) (functorial in \(S\), subject to the conditions expressed by the commutativity of the diagrams (2.0.5) and (2.0.6)), and with a structure of extension of \(P_Q\) by \(G_Q\), defined by isomorphisms (2.0.7) (functorial in \(S\), subject to the conditions expressed by the commutativity of the diagrams (2.0.8) and (2.0.9)). We say that the two preceding structures are compatible if for every object \(S\) of \(T\) and for \(p,p'\in P(S)\), \(q,q'\in Q(S)\), we have commutativity in the diagram

\[(2.1.1)\qquad \xymatrix@C=4em@R=2em{ &E_{p,qq'}E_{p',qq'}\ar[ddr]^{\varphi_{p,p';qq'}}&\\ E_{p,q}E_{p,q'}E_{p',q}E_{p',q'}\ar[d]_{\mathrm{sym}}\ar[ur]^{\psi_{p;q,q'}\,\psi_{p';q,q'}}&&\\ E_{p,q}E_{p',q}E_{p,q'}E_{p',q'}\ar[dr]_{\varphi_{p,p';q}\,\varphi_{p,p';q'}}&&E_{pp',qq'}\\ &E_{pp',q}E_{pp',q'}\ar[ur]_{\psi_{pp';q,q'}}& }\]

where the vertical arrow is deduced from the symmetry isomorphism \(E_{p,q'}E_{p',q}\xrightarrow{\sim}E_{p',q}E_{p,q'}\). We call a biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\) a torsor \(E\) under \(G_{P\times Q}\), endowed with a structure of extension of \(Q_P\) by \(G_P\) and a structure of extension of \(P_Q\) by \(G_Q\), these two structures being compatible in the preceding sense.

2.1.1. We may also say that a biextension structure on the \(G_{P\times Q}\)-torsor \(E\) is defined by two not-everywhere-defined composition laws \(\varphi(x,y)\) and \(\psi(x,y)\) on \(E\), the first being defined when \(\operatorname{pr}_2j(x)=\operatorname{pr}_2j(y)\), the second when \(\operatorname{pr}_1j(x)=\operatorname{pr}_1j(y)\) (where \(j:E\longrightarrow P\times Q\) is the structural morphism of the torsor). The axioms, in addition to the associativities of the type
\[(2.1.1.1)\qquad \varphi(gx,y)=\varphi(x,gy)=g\varphi(x,y) \qquad(x,y\in E(S),\ g\in G(S))\]
and the analogous relations in \(\psi\), are then expressed by the associativity and the commutativity of these partial composition laws, and by the relation
\[(2.1.1.2)\qquad \varphi(\psi(x,y),\psi(z,t))=\psi(\varphi(x,z),\varphi(y,t))\]
for \(x,y,z,t\) respectively in \(E_{p,q}(S),E_{p,q'}(S),E_{p',q}(S),E_{p',q'}(S)\).
2.2. Unit sections.
It is appropriate to consider the unit section of \(E\), considered as an extension of \(Q_P\) by \(G_P\),
\[(2.2.1)\qquad e_{E/P}\in\Gamma(E/P)=\operatorname{Hom}_P(P,E),\]
and symmetrically the unit section of \(E\), considered as an extension of \(P_Q\) by \(G_Q\),
\[(2.2.2)\qquad e_{E/Q}\in\Gamma(E/Q)=\operatorname{Hom}_Q(Q,E).\]

In particular, the torsor \(E\) above \(P\times Q\) (with Group \(G_{P\times Q}\)) is canonically trivialized above \(1_P\times Q\) by \(e_{E/P}\), and above \(P\times1_Q\) by \(e_{E/Q}\). Moreover, these trivializations coincide above the unit section \(1_P\times1_Q\) of \(P\times Q\), and therefore define one and the same section
\[(2.2.3)\qquad e_E\in\Gamma(E),\]

which we will call by abuse of language the unit section (or neutral section) of \(E\). To prove the announced equality of the two sections \(e,e'\) of \(E\) deduced respectively from \(e_{E/P}\) and \(e_{E/Q}\), one notes that they are characterized respectively as the unit sections in the inverse image \(E_{1_P,1_Q}\) in \(E\) of the unit section of \(P\times Q\), for the two composition laws \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\). Applying then (2.1.1.2) for the sections \(e,e',e',e\) of \(E_{1,1}\), one finds that the two members are equal respectively to \(e\) and \(e'\), whence \(e=e'\) as announced. One concludes from this moreover that the two composition laws \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) on \(E_{1,1}\) are identical. Indeed, it is immediate that they correspond to the Group law of \(G\) by transport of structure, with the help of the morphisms \(g\longmapsto g.e\) and \(g\longmapsto g.e'\) respectively, hence they are identical since \(e=e'\).

Let us also note that the extension \(E\) of \(Q_P\) by \(G_P\) over the base \(P\) gives, by restriction above the section \(1_P\) of \(P\), a structure of extension of \(Q\) by \(G\) on the inverse image of \(1_P\times Q\) in \(G\). One observes at once that the homomorphism \(e_{E/Q}\) is a multiplicative homomorphism for this extension structure, which is therefore canonically split. In a symmetric way, the extension of \(P\) by \(G\) obtained by restriction of the extension \(E\) of \(P_Q\) by \(G_Q\) on \(Q\) above the section \(1_Q\) of \(Q\), is canonically split by \(e_{E/P}\).
2.3. Morphisms of biextensions.

Let \(E\) be a biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), and likewise \(E'\) a biextension of \((P',Q')\) by \(G'\), where \(P',Q',G'\) are again commutative Groups of \(T\). We call a morphism of biextensions from \(E\) to \(E'\) a system \((u,v,w,f)\), where
\[(2.3.1)\qquad u:P\longrightarrow P',\quad v:Q\longrightarrow Q',\quad w:G\longrightarrow G'\]
are morphisms of Groups, and where
\[(2.3.2)\qquad f:E\longrightarrow E'\]

is a morphism of the sheaves of sets underlying \(E,E'\), in such a way that \(f\) be at once a homomorphism of extensions of Groups over \(P\) resp. \(P'\) associated to the homomorphisms of relative Groups
\[u\times v:Q_P\longrightarrow Q'_{P'},\qquad u\times w:G_P\longrightarrow G'_{P'},\]
and a homomorphism of extensions of Groups over \(Q\) resp. \(Q'\) associated to the homomorphisms of relative Groups
\[u\times v:P_Q\longrightarrow P'_{Q'},\qquad v\times w:G_Q\longrightarrow G'_{Q'}.\]

It will moreover be noted that \(u,v,w\) are known from the knowledge of \(f\) alone, for from \(f\) one recovers \(u\times v\) by passage to the quotient, hence also \(u\) and \(v\) by restriction to \(P\times1_Q\) resp. \(1_P\times Q\), and one recovers \(w\) by restriction of \(f\) to \(G.e_E\). So there is no harm in identifying the morphism \((u,v,w,f)\) with \(f\).

With the usual precaution of adding to the morphisms their source and their target (canonical genuflection), one sees therefore that the biextensions of the topos \(T\) are the objects of a category whose objects and morphisms are those defined in 2.1 and 2.3, and where the composition
of morphisms is defined in the obvious way. Moreover, by \((P,Q,G,E)\longmapsto(P,Q,G)\) one obtains a canonical functor
\[(2.3.3)\qquad \mathbf{BIEXT}(T)\longrightarrow \mathbf{GRAB}(T)\times\mathbf{GRAB}(T)\times\mathbf{GRAB}(T),\]

where \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(T)\) denotes the category of biextensions on \(T\), and \(\mathbf{GRAB}(T)\) that of abelian Groups on \(T\). It is moreover appropriate to decompose the preceding canonical functor into two, namely \(E\longmapsto(P,Q)\):
\[(2.3.4)\qquad \mathbf{BIEXT}(T)\longrightarrow \mathbf{GRAB}(T)\times\mathbf{GRAB}(T),\]
and \(E\longmapsto G\):
\[(2.3.5)\qquad \mathbf{BIEXT}(T)\longrightarrow\mathbf{GRAB}(T).\]
2.4. The category \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q,G)\) for variable \(P,Q,G\).

I claim that the canonical functor (2.3.4) is fibering, and the canonical functor (2.3.5) is cofibering. The first assertion means therefore in particular that for a biextension \(E\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), and for morphisms of abelian Groups
\[u:P'\longrightarrow P,\qquad v:Q'\longrightarrow Q,\]
one can find a biextension \(E'\) of \((P',Q')\) by \(G\), and a morphism of biextensions
\[f:E'\longrightarrow E\]

compatible with \(u,v,\operatorname{id}_G\), which enjoys the usual universal property for morphisms of biextensions into \(E\) compatible with \(u,v,\operatorname{id}_G\). The second assertion is spelled out likewise, and means in particular that for a biextension \(E\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), and a morphism of abelian Groups
\[w:G\longrightarrow G',\]
there exists a biextension \(E'\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G'\), and a morphism of biextensions
\[g:E\longrightarrow E'\]

compatible with \(\operatorname{id}_P,\operatorname{id}_Q\) and \(w\), which enjoys the usual universal property for morphisms of biextensions from \(E\) compatible with \(\operatorname{id}_P,\operatorname{id}_Q\) and \(w\). We leave to the reader the task of making explicit the two constructions whose existence we assert here, constructions which are easily carried out by simply paraphrasing the well-known analogous constructions in the case of extensions of ordinary Groups.
Thus, denoting, for three abelian Groups \(P,Q,G\) of \(T\), by
\[(2.4.1)\qquad \mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\]

the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), defined as the fiber category of (2.3.3) at \((P,Q,G)\) (thus taking as morphisms therein the morphisms of biextensions which are compatible with the identity morphisms of \(P,Q,G\)), one sees that this category "depends covariantly on \(G\), and contravariantly on \(P,Q\)". Moreover, for the preceding variances, the category \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\) depends additively on each of the three arguments \(P,Q,G\). Let us make explicit the precise meaning of this assertion, for the argument \(G\) for example:

1. a) \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;0)\) is equivalent to the punctual category, and
  2. b) for two abelian Groups \(G,G'\) of \(T\), the functor
\[(2.4.2)\qquad \mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G\times G')\longrightarrow \mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\times\mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G')\]

deduced from the two projections \(G\times G'\longrightarrow G\) and \(G\times G'\longrightarrow G'\) is an equivalence of categories. In the language of [4], one also says that for fixed \(P,Q\), the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by variable Groups \(G\) is,
via the functor \(E\longmapsto G\) induced by (2.3.5), an additive cofibered category over the category \(\mathbf{GRAB}(T)\).

Remark 2.4.3. With the terminology of loc. cit., the preceding cofibered category is moreover not only additive, but even "left exact" [4, 2.2]. We will not verify this property here; we will recover it below (3.5) as a consequence of more precise facts. Let us only point out that the general theory of loc. cit. would then allow us to make explicit the structure of this cofibered category in terms of a chain complex \(L_\bullet\) in \(\mathbf{GRAB}(T)\) (depending only on \(P\) and \(Q\)), up to equivalence of fibered categories. We will also find below a direct construction of this complex \(L_\bullet\) as being the complex \(P\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathrm L}}Q\) (tensor product taken in a derived category).
2.5. Structure of the category \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\).
The general formal considerations of [4, n° 1] allow one to draw some useful consequences from the fact that the categories \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\) depend additively on \(G\).

2.5.1. First of all, for fixed \(G\), the category \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\) is endowed with a composition structure, allowing one to associate functorially to two biextensions \(E,E'\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\) a product biextension, denoted \(E.E'\). One may consider that this construction merely paraphrases the well-known construction of the product of two extensions of one commutative Group by another; it will moreover be noted that it is indeed compatible with this latter notion, when one interprets \(E\) and \(E'\), either as extensions of Groups of \(Q_P\) by \(G_P\), or as extensions of Groups of \(P_Q\) by \(G_Q\). In particular, \(EE'\) is, as a torsor

under \(G_{P\times Q}\), the product of the torsors under \(G_{P\times Q}\) defined by \(E\) and \(E'\). The formation of \(E.E'\) is moreover associative and commutative up to canonical isomorphisms, and the composition law envisaged in \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\) admits a "two-sided neutral object", called the trivial biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\). The latter can be constructed (loc. cit.) as the biextension deduced from the unique biextension (up to isomorphism) \(E_0\) of \((P,Q)\) by the zero Group \(0\), thanks to the homomorphism \(0\longrightarrow G\). One verifies at once on this description that the trivial biextension \(E\) can also be described as the trivial torsor
\[E=P\times Q\times G,\]

its two Group structures over \(P\) resp. over \(Q\) being deduced from the product Group structures of \(Q\times G\) resp. of \(P\times G\) by base change \(P\longrightarrow e\) resp. \(Q\longrightarrow e\).

Finally, for every biextension \(E\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), one can define the inverse biextension, denoted \(E^{-1}\), endowed with an isomorphism of \(EE^{-1}\) with the trivial biextension, which determines \(E^{-1}\) up to unique isomorphism, and allows one to define the functorial dependence with respect to \(E\).

In sum, the category \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\) appears as the analogue of a group-object of the category \((\mathbf{Cat})\) of categories, except that the associativities, commutativities, unit and inverse relations are valid only modulo given isomorphisms. We may say that this category is a pseudo-group (of the 2-category \((\mathbf{Cat})\)).

2.5.2. For fixed \(P,Q,G\), the following facts result from the preceding properties. For every biextension \(E\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), the monoid of endomorphisms of \(E\) is canonically isomorphic to the monoid of endomorphisms of the trivial biextension \(\mathbf 1\), by associating to every endomorphism \(u\) of the latter the endomorphism \(u.\operatorname{id}_E\) of \(\mathbf 1.E\simeq E\). On the other hand, the monoid of endomorphisms of \(\mathbf 1\) is a commutative group, which we will denote
\[(2.5.2.1)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^0(P,Q;G);\]

its composition law is also given by \((u,v)\longmapsto u.v\in \operatorname{End}(\mathbf 1.\mathbf 1)\simeq\operatorname{End}(\mathbf 1)\). Finally, the composition law \(E,E'\longmapsto EE'\) defines in the set
\[(2.5.2.2)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\]
of isomorphism classes of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\) a commutative group law, the inverse of the class of \(E\) being that of the inverse bitorsor \(E^{-1}\).
2.5.3. When one is given a homomorphism of Groups \(G\longrightarrow G'\), the corresponding functor
\[(2.5.3.1)\qquad \mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\longrightarrow\mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G')\]
is compatible with the laws \(E.E'\) and \(E^{-1}\) envisaged in 2.5.1, up to canonical isomorphisms. It follows in particular that the two obvious maps
\[(2.5.3.2)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^i(P,Q;G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Biext}^i(P,Q;G')\qquad(i=0,1)\]
are homomorphisms of groups. Moreover, if \(E\) is a biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), and \(E'\) the corresponding biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G'\), the natural homomorphism
\[(2.5.3.3)\qquad \operatorname{Hom}(E,E)=\operatorname{Aut}(E)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}(E',E')=\operatorname{Aut}(E')\]
is none other than (2.5.3.2) for \(i=0\).

2.5.4. One can moreover easily make explicit the group \(\operatorname{Biext}^0(P,Q;G)\). Indeed, if \(E\) is a biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), the automorphisms \(u\) of \(E\) are first of all automorphisms of the torsor under \(G_{P\times Q}\) underlying \(E\), and as such are given by the morphisms
\[u_0:P\times Q\longrightarrow G,\]
by the formula

This being settled, in order that \(u\) be compatible with the first (resp. the second) extension structure on \(E\), one sees at once that it is necessary and sufficient that \(u_0\) be additive with respect to the second (resp. with respect to the first) argument. Hence in order that \(u\) be an automorphism of the biextension structure of \(E\), it is necessary and sufficient that \(u_0\) be bilinear. There results therefore a canonical bijection
\[(2.5.4.1)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^0(P,Q;G)\simeq \operatorname{Hom}(P\otimes Q,G).\]

One easily verifies that the latter is independent of \(E\), that it respects the group structures, and that it is functorial in \(G\) (for the functorial law of the first member made explicit in (2.5.3.2)).

We will determine below the group \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\), and will find that it is canonically, and functorially in \(G\), isomorphic to \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(P\mathbin{\mathop{\otimes}^{\mathrm L}}Q,G)\).

2.6. In 2.5 we have varied only \(G\), keeping \(P\) and \(Q\) fixed. It is now fitting to say a few words concerning the variation of \(P,Q\). The essential fact, from which the others will follow formally, is that the contravariant variance of \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\) in \(P,Q\) commutes with its covariant variance in \(G\), so that the categories envisaged can be considered as the fiber categories of a cofibered category
\[(2.6.1)\qquad \mathbf{BIEXT}'(T)\longrightarrow \mathbf{GRAB}(T)^o\times\mathbf{GRAB}(T)^o\times\mathbf{GRAB}(T),\]

where the exponents \({}^o\) indicate the opposite categories, (the structural functor envisaged being \(E\longmapsto(P,Q,G)\)). As such, the cofibered category envisaged is additive in each of these three arguments, or again, as we will say, it is tri-additive. By paraphrasing standard arguments which we refrain from reproducing here, one concludes that the composition structure \(E.E'\) on the fiber category \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(P,P';G)\) can be described indifferently using the additive structure of any one of the three arguments (i.e. the three pseudo-group structures obtained are canonically isomorphic). It follows in turn that the abelian groups
\[(2.6.2)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^i(P,Q;G),\qquad i=0,1,\]

can be regarded as two tri-linear functors in the arguments \(P,Q,G\) in \(\mathbf{GRAB}(T)^o,\ \mathbf{GRAB}(T)^o\) and \(\mathbf{GRAB}(T)\), or if one prefers as trilinear functors in \(P,Q,G\) variables in \(\mathbf{GRAB}(T)\), contravariant in \(P,Q\), covariant in \(G\), with values in the category \(\mathbf{GRAB}\) of abelian groups. One verifies moreover that for this variance thus made precise, the isomorphism (2.5.4.1) is functorial in \(P,Q\) and \(G\), and not only in \(G\). The same will moreover be true below for the determination of \(\operatorname{Biext}^1\) in terms of an \(\operatorname{Ext}^1\).

2.7. Symmetric biextension of a given biextension. If \(E\) is a biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), it is appropriate to define the symmetric biextension \({}^sE\) of \(E\), which is a biextension of \((Q,P)\) by \(G\). The sheaf of sets underlying \({}^sE\) is that underlying \(E\), the operations of \(G\) on \(E\) and on \({}^sE\) are the same, the structural morphism \({}^sE\longrightarrow P\times Q\) is the composite of the structural morphism \(E\longrightarrow P\times Q\) with the symmetry \(P\times Q\longrightarrow Q\times P\), and finally the left and right extension structures of \({}^sE\) are respectively the right and left extension structures of \(E\). It is evident that the operation \(E\longmapsto{}^sE\) is an automorphism of the category \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(T)\) of biextensions, and in fact an involutive automorphism:

Every statement concerning bitorsors \(E,\ldots\) allows one to deduce a symmetric statement, by applying the given statement to \({}^sE,\ldots\). It may be called the statement deduced "by exchanging left and right". Of course, one will in general content oneself with spelling out only one of the two symmetric statements, choosing between one's left and one's right, following for example one's political preferences.
2.8. Change of topos. Localization. Suppose given a morphism of topoi
\[f:T'\longrightarrow T.\]

Then the "inverse image" functor \(f^*\) transforms a biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\) into a biextension \(f^*(E)\) of \((f^*(P),f^*(Q))\) by \(f^*(G)\), as is the case each time one has a species of structure which can be made explicit "in terms of finite projective limits and arbitrary inductive limits". To show the compatibility of the two extension structures on \(f^*(E)\) deduced from those of \(E\), one will express this compatibility by the commutativity of the diagram (2.1.1) in the "universal" case, i.e. for \(S=P\times P\times Q\times Q\), \(p,p',q,q'\) being the four projections of \(S\) onto these four factors. As is proper, one will obtain by \(E\longmapsto f^*(E)\) a canonical functor
\[f^*:\mathbf{BIEXT}(T)\longrightarrow\mathbf{BIEXT}(T').\]

We will use in the sequel without further mention the fact that all the constructions which we may be led to carry out by means of biextensions are compatible with inverse images by morphisms of topoi. This is in particular the case for the operations of product \(EE'\) and of passage to the inverse \(E^{-1}\), considered in 2.5.
One may take in particular for \(T'\) an induced topos \(T_{/S'}\), and for \(f\) the canonical morphism (called the "localization" morphism (SGA 4 IV 5))
\[f=i_{S'}:T_{/S'}\longrightarrow T.\]

One then calls \(f^*(E)\) the biextension induced by \(E\) on \(S'\), and it will be denoted also, as usual, \(E|_{S'}\) or \(E_{S'}\). As the restriction operation evidently possesses the usual transitivity properties, one concludes that the categories \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(T_{/S})\), for a variable object \(S\) of \(T\), form the fibers of a fibered category over \(T\) (SGA 1 VI 8). In fact, one verifies trivially that this category is even a stack over \(T\) in the sense of Giraud [2], i.e. that "the morphisms and the objects of the fibers of this fibered category can be glued". For example, to give a biextension up to canonical isomorphism on \(S\), it suffices to give a covering (i.e. epimorphic) family \(S_i\longrightarrow S\), for every \(i\in I\) a biextension \(E_i\) on \(S_i\) (i.e. in \(T_{/S_i}\)), for every pair \((i,j)\) an isomorphism of \(E_i|_{S_j}\) with \(E_j|_{S_i}\), these isomorphisms being subject to the usual cocycle condition. We will call the stack just defined the stack of biextensions over \(T\). The functor (2.3.3), hence also the deduced functors (2.3.4) and (2.3.5), when one replaces \(T\) therein by \(T_{/S}\) with \(S\) variable, correspond to morphisms of stacks over \(T\). Likewise the operations \(E.E'\) and \(E^{-1}\) (2.5.1) in the categories \(\mathbf{BIEXT}(P_S,Q_S;G_S)\) for variable \(S\), \(P,Q,G\) being given commutative Groups over \(T\).
2.9. Relations with birigidified torsors

With the terminology of 1.3.2, we have seen in 2.2 that for every biextension \(E\) of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), the structure of torsor under \(G_{P\times Q}\) underlying \(E\) is canonically birigidified relative to the pair of unit sections of \(P\) and of \(Q\). One thus finds a canonical functor
\[(2.9.1)\qquad \mathbf{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)\xrightarrow{\ i\ } \mathbf{TORSBIRIG}(P,Q;G),\]
where the second member denotes the category of birigidified torsors on \(P\times Q\) (under \(G_{P\times Q}\)).

Using the unit sections of \(P\) and of \(Q\), one finds, for every product of \(n\) objects isomorphic to \(P\) or \(Q\), a canonical morphism \(\operatorname{inj}_i\) of the \(i\)-th factor into the said product. If one is given a morphism of this product into \(G\), one may thus speak of the restriction of this morphism to the \(i\)-th factor. This being settled, we consider the products
\[(2.9.2)\qquad \left\{\begin{array}{l} P\times Q\\ P\times Q\times Q,\ P\times Q\times Q\times Q\\ P\times P\times Q,\ P\times P\times P\times Q\\ P\times P\times Q\times Q \end{array}\right.\ ,\]

Proposition 2.9.3. Suppose that every morphism of one of the products (2.9.2) into \(G\) which is trivial on each of the factors of this product (embedded in the latter thanks to the unit sections as has just been explained) is trivial. Then:

1. a) The canonical functor (2.9.1) is fully faithful, and the two members of 2.9.1 are rigid categories. In particular, if \(E\) is a torsor under \(G_{P\times Q}\) birigidified for \((e_P,e_Q)\), there exists on \(E\) at most one biextension structure compatible with its birigidified torsor structure.

1. b) In order that there exist such a biextension structure on \(E\), it is necessary and sufficient that there exist on \(E\) a structure of extension of \(Q_P\) by \(G_P\) compatible with its structure of torsor under \(G_P\), and that there exist on \(E\) a structure of extension of \(P_Q\) by \(G_Q\) compatible with its structure of torsor under \(G_Q\) (cf. 1.3.5 b)). There exists in this case on \(E\) a unique structure of extension of \(Q_P\) by \(G_P\) compatible with the structures of torsor under \(G_P\) and the given rigidification on \(P\times e_Q\), and a unique structure of extension of \(P_Q\) by \(G_Q\) compatible with the structure of torsor under \(G_Q\) and the given rigidification on \(e_P\times Q\), and these two extension structures are compatible (2.1).

Proof. a) If \(E\) is a birigidified torsor on \(P\times Q\), the group of its automorphisms is identified with the group of morphisms \(P\times Q\longrightarrow G\) which are trivial on the two partial factors, hence by the hypothesis applied to the product \(P\times Q\), it is the identity automorphism. That proves the rigidity assertion. To show that if \(E,E'\) are biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), every morphism of birigidified bitorsors \(f:E\longrightarrow E'\) is a morphism of biextensions, it suffices to apply 1.3.5 a) to the two extension structures defined by \(E,E'\) on \(P\) resp. on \(Q\) (which leads to using the hypothesis for the two products \(P\times Q\times Q\) and \(P\times P\times Q\) of (2.9.2)).

b) The necessity of the condition stated on the birigidified torsor \(E\) in order that it come from a biextension being clear, let us prove the sufficiency. It follows first from 1.3.5 b) that the condition stated implies the existence of unique extension structures of \(Q_P\) by \(G_P\) and of \(P_Q\) by \(G_Q\), compatible with the torsor structure and the rigidifications. (One uses the hypothesis for the products \(P\times Q\times Q\times Q\) and \(P\times P\times P\times Q\) in (2.9.2)). It remains to prove that these two extension structures are compatible, i.e. that we have commutativity in the diagram (2.1.1). Now the two composites of this diagram are isomorphisms of torsors on \(P\times P\times Q\times Q\), hence they differ by a morphism
\[u:P\times P\times Q\times Q\longrightarrow G.\]

Using the fact that the partial trivializations of \(E\) are unit sections for the laws \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) respectively, one easily concludes that \(u\) is trivial on each of these four factors, hence by the hypothesis applied to the product \(P\times P\times Q\times Q\) of (2.9.2), that it is trivial, which completes the proof.

Corollary 2.9.4.

Let \(P,Q,G\) be commutative Groups satisfying the relations

\[(2.9.4.1)\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{pt}(P,G)=e,\qquad \underline{\operatorname{Hom}}_{pt}(Q,G)=e\]

(cf. 1.3.7). Then the preliminary conditions of 2.9.2 are satisfied, hence the conclusions a) and b) of that proposition are valid. Moreover, if \(E\) is a torsor under \(G_{P\times Q}\) birigidified for \((e_P,e_Q)\), in order that it admit a structure of biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\) compatible with its given structures, it is necessary and sufficient that the two morphisms of pointed sheaves of sets which it defines,

\[(2.9.4.2)\qquad Q\longrightarrow R^1p_*(G_P),\qquad P\longrightarrow R^1q_*(G_Q)\]

(\(p,q\) the structural morphisms \(P\longrightarrow e\) and \(Q\longrightarrow e\)), be morphisms of Groups.

The first assertion of 2.9.4 was seen in 1.3.7. For the second, the necessity of the condition envisaged being clear, let us prove the sufficiency, by applying 2.9.3 b) and 1.3.5 b). Let us note that the multiplicativity of the first morphism of (2.9.4.2) means that the rigidified torsor \(E\) under \(G_P\) satisfies the condition of 1.3.5 b) locally on \(P\). We have already pointed out in 1.3.7 that this implies that this condition is true globally on \(P\), hence that on \(E\) there exists a structure of extension of \(Q_P\) by \(G_P\) compatible with its structure of rigidified torsor under \(G_P\). One proceeds likewise to see that on \(E\) there exists a structure of extension of \(P_Q\) by \(G_Q\) compatible with its structure of rigidified torsor under \(G_Q\). The conclusion therefore follows from 2.9.3 b).

Example 2.9.5. Let \(S\) be a scheme, \(P\) and \(Q\) two abelian schemes over \(S\), and \(G=G_{mS}\) the multiplicative group scheme over \(S\). It was pointed out in 1.3.8 that then the conditions (2.9.4.1) are satisfied. On the other hand, the datum of a birigidified torsor \(E\) on \(P\times Q\) then amounts to the datum of a birigidified invertible Module on \(P\times Q\). Let us recall that one calls a divisorial correspondence on \(P,Q\) the class up to isomorphism of such a birigidified invertible Module (cf. for example [1]). Working with the fppf topology, the morphisms (2.9.4.2) are here the morphisms
\[(2.9.5.1)\qquad Q\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Pic}}_{P/S},\qquad P\longrightarrow \underline{\operatorname{Pic}}_{Q/S},\]

defined by the divisorial correspondence, and it is well known by the "theorem of the square" (loc. cit.) that these morphisms are necessarily compatible with the Group structures. Hence 2.9.4 teaches us that the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G=G_{mS}\) is equivalent to the category of birigidified invertible Modules on \(P\times Q\), and that it is therefore a rigid category whose set of classes up to isomorphism is identified with the set of divisorial correspondences on \((P,Q)\).
This identification moreover respects, manifestly, the natural group laws on these two sets (the first being defined in (2.5.2.2)).

Remark 2.9.6. One can easily verify that the additivity of the first morphism (2.9.4.2) is equivalent to saying that the second morphism (2.9.4.2) factors through the subsheaf \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G)\) (note that by virtue of 1.3.5, the canonical morphism
\[\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G)\longrightarrow R^1q_*(G_Q)\]

is injective). Likewise the additivity of the second morphism (2.9.4.2) means that the first morphism factors through \(\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(P,G)\). Moreover, one obtains in this way isomorphisms of groups

\[(2.9.6.1)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}(P,Q;G)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(Q,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(P,G)) \mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(P,\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G)).\]
In the particular case envisaged in 2.9.5, these isomorphisms reduce to the well-known isomorphisms
\[(2.9.6.2)\qquad \operatorname{Corr}(P,Q)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Hom}(P,Q')\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}\operatorname{Hom}(Q,P'),\]

where \(P'=\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(P,G_m)\) and \(Q'=\underline{\operatorname{Ext}}^1(Q,G_m)\) are the dual abelian schemes of \(P\) and of \(Q\), which are identified respectively with \(\underline{\operatorname{Pic}}^0_{P/S}\) and \(\underline{\operatorname{Pic}}^0_{Q/S}\). In the two preceding formulas, the Hom denote of course the groups of homomorphisms of Groups.
2.10. Various generalizations of the notion of biextension.
We point them out for conscience's sake, for we do not have to use them.

2.10.1. One can define the notion of biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\) without supposing \(P\) or \(Q\) commutative, \(G\) alone being supposed commutative: it is a torsor under \(G_{P\times Q}\), endowed with structures of central extension of \(Q_P\) by \(G_P\) and of \(P_Q\) by \(G_Q\), these two structures being compatible with the structure of torsor, and compatible with each other i.e. making the diagrams (2.1.1) commutative. Most of the preceding developments are valid mutatis mutandis for this more general notion. One will take care that when \(P\) and \(Q\) are also commutative, the preceding notion is more general than that of 2.1, which corresponds to the case where the two extension structures underlying \(E\) are structures of commutative relative Groups. One will take care also that in the non-commutative case, the category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\) still depends additively on \(G\), but not on \(P\) and \(Q\).

2.10.2. By paraphrasing 2.1, one defines (for a given integer \(n\geq1\)) the notion of n-extension of a sequence of n commutative Groups \((P_1,P_2,\ldots,P_n)\) by a commutative Group \(G\). It corresponds to the datum, on a torsor \(E\) under \(G_{P_1\times\cdots\times P_n}\), of \(n\) partial extension structures on the partial products of \(n-1\) factors, with a compatibility relation of the type (2.1.1) for each pair of distinct indices between 1 and \(n\).

When \(n\geq3\) and the \(P_i\) are abelian schemes over a scheme \(S\), \(G\) being the multiplicative group over \(G_m\) (compare 2.9.2), one finds that every n-extension of \((P_1,\ldots,P_n)\) by \(G\) is trivial.

2.10.3. The notion of biextension 2.1 may be considered as a variant of the notion of commutative extension of commutative Groups, i.e. of the notion of extension of \(\mathbb Z\)-Modules. When one is given a Ring (commutative for simplicity) \(A\) of \(T\), and three \(A\)-Modules \(P,Q,G\), one can

define more generally the notion of biextension of A-Modules of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), which is a variant of that of extension of A-Modules. Such a biextension is defined as a biextension in the sense of 2.1, but with the underlying partial extension structures enriched into structures of extensions of \(A_Q\)-Modules resp. of \(A_P\)-Modules (compatible with the relative Module structures which one already has on \(Q_P,G_P\) and \(P_Q,G_Q\)). The two relative Module structures on \(E\) can be interpreted in terms of two operations of the multiplicative monoid underlying \(A\) on the sheaf of sets underlying \(E\), and one supposes that these two actions commute with each other; this compatibility is thus added to that expressed by the commutativity of the diagram (2.1.1), and is expressed by the commutativity of an analogous diagram, which we leave to the reader the task of writing. Finally, one supposes that the first operation of \(A\) on \(E\) be distributive, not only with respect to the first composition law of \(E\), but also for the second composition law of \(E\); and likewise for the second operation of \(A\) on \(E\).

It will be observed that the dictionary made explicit in 1.1.6 also admits an A-linear variant, which may be expressed by saying that the datum of an extension of the A-Module \(P\) by another \(G\) amounts to the datum of a family of torsors \(E_p\) under the \(G_S\), parametrized by the "points" \(p\in P(S)\) (\(S\in\operatorname{ob}T\) being a variable object), \(E_p\) being required to vary in an A-linear way (and not only additively as in 1.1.6) in \(p\). This is spelled out by the datum, in addition to the isomorphisms \(\varphi_{p,p'}\) (1.1.3.1), of the datum of isomorphisms
\[\mu_{a,p}:a^*(P_p)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow}P_{ap} \qquad(p\in P(S),\ a\in A(S)),\]

where the first member denotes the image of the \(G_S\)-torsor \(P_p\) under the extension of scalars \(g\longmapsto ag:G_S\longrightarrow G_S\). We leave to the reader the task of making explicit the conditions to impose on these data \(\varphi,\mu\) in order that they correspond indeed to an extension of Modules \(E\) of \(P\) by \(G\) (cf 3.5.4 b)).
