3. Complements of homological algebra
The principal aim of the present section is the definition of the isomorphism
\[\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\mathrel{\widetilde\longrightarrow} \operatorname{Ext}^1(P\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^{L}}Q,G)\]

announced above. We will need for this certain developments of homological algebra, having an independent interest, which will occupy sections 3.1 to 3.4. The developments 3.1 and 3.3 may be considered as complements to [4], and notably to section 6.9 of loc. cit.
3.1. Construction of an additive cofibered category \(\Psi_L\) in terms of a chain complex \(L\), in an abelian category \(C\).
Let \(C\) be an abelian category, \(L\) a chain complex of \(C\),
\[(3.1.1)\qquad L_2\longrightarrow L_1\longrightarrow L_0\longrightarrow0,\]

which we will write with the lower-degree convention (differential operator of degree -1). We thus have \(L_i=0\) for \(i<0\). We are going to define a cofibered category \(\Psi=\Psi_L\) over \(C\) (which, up to canonical equivalence of cofibered categories, will be the category of the same name of loc. cit. 6.9). The objects of \(\Psi_L\) are pairs
where \(X\) is an extension of \(L_0\) by an object \(A\) of \(C\) (depending on \(X\))
\[(3.1.2)\qquad 0\longrightarrow A\mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{i}}X \mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{j}}L_0\longrightarrow0,\]

and where \(\alpha\) is a trivialization of the inverse image \(d_0^*(X)\) of this extension by \(d_0:L_1\longrightarrow L_0\), \(\alpha\) being subject to the condition that the corresponding trivialization of the inverse image \(d_1^*(d_0^*(X))\) by \(d_1:L_2\longrightarrow L_1\) is the obvious trivialization, resulting from the transitivity isomorphism

and from the relation \(d_0d_1=0\). Another way of expressing the datum of \(\alpha\), subject to the preceding condition, is to say that one gives oneself a lifting of \(d_0:L_1\longrightarrow L_0\) to \(\alpha:L_1\longrightarrow X\), subject to the condition \(\alpha d_1=0\):
\[(3.1.3)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3.2em@R=2em{ &&0\ar[d]\\ &&A\ar[d]^i\\ &&X\ar[d]^j\\ L_2\ar[r]^{d_1}&L_1\ar[ur]^\alpha\ar[r]_{d_0}&L_0\ar[d]\\ &&0 }\qquad,\qquad \alpha d_1=0.\tag{*}\]

In this form, one sees that the description of the objects \((X,\alpha)\) depends only on the chain complex \([L_{\bullet}]\) truncated at order 1, deduced from \(L_{\bullet}\) by "killing the \(H_i(L_{\bullet})\) for \(i\geq2\)", whose components are zero in dimension \(\geq2\), coincide with those of \(L_{\bullet}\) in dimension \(\leq0\), and whose component in dimension 1 is the cokernel of \(d_1:L_2\longrightarrow L_1\). One defines the homomorphisms from \((X,\alpha)\) to \((X',\alpha')\) as the homomorphisms of extensions \(u:X\longrightarrow X'\), compatible with the identity on \(L_0\), such that \(u\alpha=\alpha'\); one composes the homomorphisms by composing the corresponding homomorphisms of extensions. One thus finds the announced category \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}\). It depends, up to canonical isomorphism, only on the order-1 truncation \([L_{\bullet}]\) of \(L_{\bullet}\).
We have an obvious functor, \((X,\alpha)\longmapsto A\):
\[(3.1.4)\qquad \pi:\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}\longrightarrow C.\]
This functor is cofibering. Indeed, let
\[u:A\longrightarrow B\]

be a morphism of \(C\), and \((X,\alpha)\) an object of the fiber category \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\) at \(A\). Thus \(X\) is an extension of \(L_0\) by \(A\), and one can consider the extension \(u_*(X)\) of \(L_0\) by \(B\), direct image of \(X\) by \(u\), and endow it with the composite partial trivialization \(\beta=f\alpha\), where \(f:X\longrightarrow u_*(X)\) is the canonical morphism. One then verifies immediately that the morphism
\[f:(X,\alpha)\longrightarrow(u_*(X),\beta)\]

is a cocartesian morphism for the functor (3.1.4), i.e. satisfies the usual universal property for the \(u\)-morphisms in \(\Psi_L\) with source \((X,\alpha)\); for this it suffices to use the analogous universal property for the morphism \(f:X\longrightarrow u_*(X)\), which is well known and trivial.
Finally, the cofibered category \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}\) is additive, i.e. it satisfies the two conditions:

\[(3.1.5)\qquad \Psi_{L_{\bullet}}(0)\text{ is equivalent to the punctual category},\] \[(3.1.6)\qquad \Psi_{L_{\bullet}}(A\times B)\longrightarrow \Psi_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\times\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}(B)\text{ is an equivalence},\]

for any two objects \(A\) and \(B\) of \(C\). The verification is trivial, in terms of the corresponding properties for the cofibered category of extensions of \(L_0\) by variable objects of \(C\). We will freely use the consequences of the additivity of \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}\), established in (loc. cit. 1) and recalled above (2.5).

3.1.7. Among these consequences, let us mention the fact that for every object \(A\) of \(C\), there is in \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\) a zero, or trivial, object (2.5.1); one verifies that it is defined by the trivial extension \(X=L_0+A\) of \(L_0\) by \(A\), endowed with the partial trivialization defined by \(\alpha:L_1\longrightarrow X\) with components \(d_0\) and 0. On the other hand (2.5.2), for every object \((X,\alpha)\) of \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\), the group of automorphisms of this object is canonically isomorphic to the group of automorphisms of the trivial object of \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\), namely \(\Psi^0_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\), which is a commutative group. Of course, one trivially recovers this fact here on the explicit description of \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\), and one even finds a canonical isomorphism

\[(3.1.8)\qquad \Psi^0_{L_{\bullet}}(A)= \operatorname{Ker}(\operatorname{Hom}(L_0,A)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Hom}(L_1,A)) \simeq\operatorname{Hom}(H_0(L_{\bullet}),A) \simeq\operatorname{Ext}^0(L_{\bullet},A),\]
induced by the usual isomorphism of the group of extension automorphisms of \(X\) with the group \(\operatorname{Hom}(L_0,A)\). The isomorphism (3.1.7.1) is evidently functorial in \(A\).

3.1.9. Let us finally recall (2.5.2) that thanks to the natural multiplicative structure in the fibered category \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\), the set of isomorphism classes of the latter is endowed in a natural way with a group structure, which we will denote \(\Psi^1_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\), and that the latter is an additive functor with respect to \(A\).
3.2. Determination of \(\Psi^1_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\)
Let us consider an object \((X,\alpha)\) (3.1.3) of \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\). Let us consider the cochain complex of length 1
\[(3.2.1)\qquad [X\longrightarrow L_0]:\quad 0\longrightarrow X\longrightarrow L_0\longrightarrow0\cdots\]

defined by the morphism \(j:X\longrightarrow L_0\), the component of degree 0 being \(X\); it is a resolution of \(A\), hence may be identified with \(A\) as an object of the derived category \(D(C)\) [5]. This being settled, the diagram (3.1.3) furnishes us a homomorphism of degree 1 of complexes
\[(3.2.2)\qquad L_{\bullet}\longrightarrow[X\longrightarrow L_0],\]
made explicit in the diagram with anticommutative squares

\[(3.2.3)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2em{ \cdots\ar[r]&L_2\ar[r]^{d_1}&L_1\ar[r]^{d_0}\ar[d]_{u^{-1}=-\alpha}&L_0\ar[r]\ar[d]^{u^0=\mathrm{id}}&0\ar[r]&\cdots\\ \cdots\ar[r]&0\ar[r]&X\ar[r]^j&L_0\ar[r]&0\ar[r]&\cdots }\]

(We will use the usual convention, associating to the chain complex \(L_{\bullet}\) a complex with differential operator of degree 1, whose component of degree \(i\) is \(L_{-i}\)). One thus finds a homomorphism of degree 1 in the derived category
\[(3.2.3)\qquad c(X,\alpha):L_{\bullet}\longrightarrow A \quad\text{(hom. of degree 1 in \(D(C)\))},\]
which manifestly depends only on the isomorphism class of \((X,\alpha)\). We have thus defined a canonical map
\[(3.2.4)\qquad \Psi^1_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}^1(L_{\bullet},A)\mathrel{\mathop=^{\mathrm{dfn}}} \operatorname{Hom}_{D(C)}(L_{\bullet},A[1]).\]
Theorem 3.2.5. The map (3.2.4) is an isomorphism of groups, functorial in the object \(A\) of \(C\).

We will leave to the reader the task of establishing the additivity of the map (3.2.4), and its functoriality for \(A\), and we will confine ourselves to proving that it is bijective. For the injectivity, let us first show that if the homomorphism of complexes \(u\) of (3.2.3) is zero in \(D(C)\), then it is already zero in the category \(K(C)\) of complexes up to homotopy, i.e. there exists a morphism
\[s:L_0\longrightarrow X\]
such that we have
\[(3.2.5.1)\qquad js=u^0=\mathrm{id}_{L_0},\qquad sd_0=-u^{-1}=\alpha.\]
Indeed, the hypothesis means that the conclusion becomes true after composing the morphism of complexes envisaged with a suitable quasi-isomorphism \(v\)
\[(*)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2em{ 0\ar[r]&X\ar[r]^j\ar[d]&L_0\ar[r]\ar[d]&0\\ 0\ar[r]&C^0\ar[r]^{j'}&C^1\ar[r]&C^2\ar[r]&\cdots, }\]

where one may moreover (replacing if necessary \(C^i\) by 0 for \(i\geq2\), by \(\operatorname{Im}(C^0\longrightarrow C^1)=\operatorname{Ker}(C^1\longrightarrow C^2)\) for \(i=1\)) suppose that \(C^i=0\) for \(i\geq2\). But then, the morphism (*) inducing the identity on \(A\), one sees that it identifies \(X\) with the fibered product of \(L_0\) and of \(C^0\) over \(C^1\), and that the datum of a morphism \(s':L_0\longrightarrow C^0\) defining a homotopy \(vu\sim0\), hence satisfying \(j's'=v^1u^0\), factors through a morphism \(s:L_0\longrightarrow X\) satisfying \(js=u^0=\mathrm{id}_{L_0}\). But then \(s\) defines a splitting of the extension \(X\) of \(L_0\) by \(A\), hence the complex \(X\longrightarrow L_0\) is isomorphic in \(K(C)\) to the complex \(K^{\bullet}\) reduced to \(A\). Now for the latter, it is evident that the homomorphism
\[\operatorname{Hom}_{K(C)}(L_{\bullet},K^{\bullet})\longrightarrow \operatorname{Hom}_{D(C)}(L_{\bullet},K^{\bullet})\]
is an isomorphism, whence our assertion.

This said, the datum of a homomorphism \(s:L_0\longrightarrow X\) satisfying the conditions (3.2.5.1) is equivalent precisely to a trivialization of the extension \(X\) of \(L_0\) by \(A\), compatible with the partial trivialization \(\alpha\). The injectivity of (3.2.4) indeed follows from this.
For the surjectivity, let us start from an element of the second member, which may therefore be described, by definition, with the help of a homomorphism of degree 1 of complexes

\[(3.2.5.2)\qquad \xymatrix@C=3em@R=2em{ \cdots\ar[r]&L_2\ar[r]^{d_1}&L_1\ar[r]^{d_0}\ar[d]_{u^{-1}}&L_0\ar[r]\ar[d]^{u^0}&0\\ &0\ar[r]&C^0\ar[r]&C^1\ar[r]&C^2\ar[r]&\cdots }\quad\text{(anticommutative squares)},\]

where \(C^{\bullet}\) is a resolution of \(A\), and where one may suppose as above that \(C^i=0\) for \(i\geq2\). Thus \(C^0\) appears as an extension of \(C^1\) by \(A\), and one can consider the extension \(X\) inverse image of the latter by \(L_0\longrightarrow C^1\). The anticommutativity of the square of (3.2.5.2) shows that \(-u^{-1}\) factors through a morphism \(\alpha:L_1\longrightarrow X\), which is a partial splitting i.e. satisfies \(j\alpha=d_0\) (\(j\) defined in (3.1.2)). Moreover the relation \(u^{-1}d_1=0\) in (3.2.5.2) implies that \(\alpha d_1=0\). Hence \((X,\alpha)\) is indeed an element of \(\Psi^1_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\). Moreover, by the very construction, the homomorphism (3.2.5.2) is the composite of the homomorphism (3.2.3) relative to \((X,\alpha)\), and of the homomorphism
\[[X\longrightarrow L_0]\longrightarrow C^{\bullet}=[C^0\longrightarrow C^1]\]

with components \((f,u^0)\), where \(f:X\longrightarrow C^0\) is the canonical homomorphism deduced from the definition of the extension \(X\) as inverse image of the extension \(C^0\). As the preceding homomorphism is a homomorphism of resolutions of \(A\) (inducing the identity on \(A\)), one concludes, by definition of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1=\operatorname{Hom}_{D(C)}^1\), that the given element of \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(L_{\bullet},A)\), described by (3.2.5.2), is none other than \(c(X,\alpha)\), which establishes the surjectivity of (3.2.4) and completes the proof of 3.2.5.
3.3. Properties of the cofibered category \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}\)
In the present section, which is not essential for the understanding of what follows, we freely use the notions of [4], our aim being to compare the preceding results with those of loc. cit.

Theorem 3.3.1. The additive cofibered category \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}\) over \(C\) is left exact (loc. cit. 2.2) it admits a quasi-maximal object (loc. cit. 4.4), and for every object \(E=(X,\alpha)\) of this category, there exists a typical complex \(L^E\) for \(E\) (loc. cit. 3.1). Moreover, the typical pro-complex of \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}\) (loc. cit. 3.7 and 4.4) is canonically isomorphic in \(D(C)\) to the truncated complex \([L_{\bullet}]\) considered in 3.1.
We make explicit below the meaning of the various assertions of 3.3.1.
3.3.2. To say that the cofibered category \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}\) is left exact means that it is additive and moreover satisfies the condition:
(3.3.2.1) For every exact sequence
\[0\longrightarrow A'\mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{u}}A \mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{v}}A''\longrightarrow0\]
of \(C\), the natural functor (notations of loc. cit. 1.8)
\[\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}(A)\longrightarrow\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}([A\longrightarrow A''])\]
is an equivalence of categories.

Recall that, for a cofibered category \(\Psi\) over \(C\), and a morphism \(v:A\longrightarrow A''\) of \(C\), \(\Psi([A\longrightarrow A''])\) denotes the category formed of the pairs \((E,\rho)\), where \(E\) is an object of \(\Psi(A)\) and where \(\rho\) is a "trivialization" of the corresponding object \(v_*(E)\) of \(\Psi(A'')\), i.e. an isomorphism of this object with the null object (2.5.1) of \(\Psi(A'')\). The definition of the functor
\[\Psi(A')\longrightarrow\Psi([A\longrightarrow A''])\]

of (3.3.2.1) is obvious. In the case \(\Psi=\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}\), the objects of \(\Psi_{L_{\bullet}}([A\longrightarrow A''])\) can be made explicit as the triples \((X,\alpha,\rho)\), where \((X,\alpha)\) is as in (3.1.3), and where \(\rho:X\longrightarrow A''\) is a morphism in \(C\) satisfying
\[(*)\qquad \rho i=v,\qquad \rho\alpha=0.\]

When \(v:A\longrightarrow A''\) fits into a short exact sequence as in 3.2.3, then the datum of a morphism satisfying the first of the relations (*) is equivalent, as is well known (left exactness of the cofibered category of extensions of \(L_0\) by variable objects of \(C\)), to the datum of an extension \(X'\) of \(L_0\) by \(A'\simeq\operatorname{Ker}v\) which is a sub-extension of \(X\) (we take \(X'=\operatorname{Ker}\rho\)), and the second relation (*) expresses that \(\alpha\) factors through an \(\alpha':L_1\longrightarrow X'\). This establishes, modulo compatibilities whose verification is obvious, the left exactness made explicit in (3.2.3).

3.3.3. Recall that an object \(E_0\) of a cofibered category \(\Psi\) over \(C\) is said to be quasi-maximal if for every object \(E\) of \(\Psi\), lying over an object \(A\) of \(C\), one can find a morphism \(E\longrightarrow E'\) in \(\Psi\), inducing a monomorphism \(A\longrightarrow A'\) in \(C\), in such a way that \(E\) is dominated by \(E_0\) i.e. that there exists a morphism \(E\longrightarrow E_0\). When in \(C\) there exist enough injective objects, it amounts to the same to say that every object of \(\Psi\) lying over an injective object of \(C\) is dominated by \(E_0\). In the case of \(\Psi=\Psi_{L_\bullet}\), we have the following canonical quasi-maximal object \(E_0\). We set
\[L'_1=\operatorname{Coker}(d_1:L_2\longrightarrow L_1)\]

and we denote by \(E_0\) the object \((X_0,\alpha_0)\) of \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}(L'_1)\), where \(X_0=L_0+L'_1\) is the trivial extension of \(L_0\) by \(L'_1\), and where \(\alpha_0\) has as components \((d'_1,\operatorname{id}_{L'_1})\), \(d'_1:L_1\longrightarrow L'_1\) being deduced from \(d_1:L_2\longrightarrow L_1\) by passage to the quotient.

To see that \(E_0\) is indeed quasi-maximal, note that for every object \((X,\alpha)\) of \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}\), one can find a morphism from it into some \((X',\alpha')\), inducing a monomorphism \(A\longrightarrow A'\) in \(C\), such that the extension \(X'\) of \(L_0\) by \(A'\) is split: it suffices in fact to take \(A'=X\), and \((X',\alpha')=i_*(X,\alpha)\), the notations being those of (3.1.1.2). On the other hand, it is immediate that an object \((X',\alpha')\) of \(\Psi\) such that the extension \(X'\) is split, is dominated by \(E_0\).
3.3.4. Let \(E\) be an object of an additive cofibered category \(\Psi\) over \(C\), lying over an object \(A\) of \(C\), and consider the functor in the variable object \(B\) of \(C\):
\[(3.3.4.1)\qquad B\longmapsto\operatorname{Hom}(E,\theta_B),\]

where \(\theta_B\) denotes the trivial object of \(\Psi(B)\). We say that \(E\) admits a typical complex if the preceding functor is representable by an object \(\Omega_E\) of \(C\). As the functor under consideration maps canonically into the functor \(B\longmapsto\operatorname{Hom}(A,B)\) represented by \(A\), we then deduce a canonical homomorphism
\[(3.3.4.2)\qquad A\longrightarrow\Omega_E\]
and the corresponding chain complex \(A\longrightarrow\Omega_E\) of \(C\) of length 1 is called the typical complex of \(E\).

In the case \(\Psi=\Psi_{L_\bullet}\), \(E=(X,\alpha)\), one verifies immediately that the functor (3.3.4.1) is representable by \(\operatorname{Coker}\alpha\), the canonical homomorphism (3.3.4.2) being none other than the composite \(A\mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{j}}X\mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{\mathrm{can}}}\operatorname{Coker}\alpha\).

3.3.5. When an additive cofibered category \(\Psi\) over \(C\) admits a quasi-maximal object (3.3.3), and the typical complexes of its objects exist, one proves that the typical complex \(T\) of a quasi-maximal object \(E_0\) of \(\Psi\), considered as an object of the derived category \(D(C)\), does not depend, up to canonical isomorphism, on the choice of \(E_0\) (loc. cit. 4.4). It is called the typical complex of the additive cofibered category \(\Psi\). When one assumes finally that \(\Psi\) is moreover left exact (3.3.2), one shows that \(\Psi\) can be reconstructed, up to equivalence of fibered categories over \(C\) (unique up to unique isomorphism), in terms of the typical complex \(T\) (loc. cit. 6.10), and that we have canonical isomorphisms functorial in the object \(A\) of \(C\) (loc. cit. 5.4):
\[(3.3.5.1)\qquad \Psi^0(A)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Ext}^0(T,A),\qquad \Psi^1(A)\xrightarrow{\sim}\operatorname{Ext}^1(T,A).\]

To finish making the link with loc. cit., we shall prove that in the case \(\Psi=\Psi_{L_\bullet}\), the typical complex of \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}\) is the truncation \([L_\bullet]\). This will give anew a definition of the isomorphisms (3.1.8) and (3.2.4) in terms of (3.3.5.1), which one verifies to be compatible with the one given in 3.1 and in 3.2.

To prove our assertion, we may obviously restrict to the case where \(L_\bullet\) is already truncated at order 1 (for we pointed out in 3.1 that truncation does not modify, up to isomorphism of cofibered categories, the category \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}\)). But then it is obvious that the typical complex of the quasi-maximal object described in 3.3.3 is canonically isomorphic to \(L_\bullet\), taking into account the explicit construction of the typical complexes given in 3.3.4.
\[0\longrightarrow A'\longrightarrow A\longrightarrow A''\longrightarrow0\]
be an exact sequence in \(C\), whence an exact sequence for the \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\):

\[0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}^0(L_\bullet,A')\longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}^0(L_\bullet,A)\longrightarrow\cdots\longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}^1(L_\bullet,A)\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}^1(L_\bullet,A''),\]
(which one could moreover continue on the right by introducing the \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) \((i\geq2)\)). By means of the isomorphisms (3.1.8) and (3.2.4) we conclude an exact sequence

\[(3.3.6.1)\qquad 0\longrightarrow\Psi^0_{L_\bullet}(A')\longrightarrow \Psi^0_{L_\bullet}(A)\longrightarrow\Psi^0_{L_\bullet}(A'') \mathrel{\mathop\longrightarrow^{\partial}}\Psi^1_{L_\bullet}(A')\longrightarrow \Psi^1_{L_\bullet}(A)\longrightarrow\Psi^1_{L_\bullet}(A''),\]

whose arrows other than the arrow denoted \(\partial\) are defined simply by functoriality of \(\Psi^0_{L_\bullet}\) and of \(\Psi^1_{L_\bullet}\). On the other hand, in loc. cit. (2.5.2), one defines an exact sequence (3.3.6.1), via a homomorphism
\[(3.3.6.2)\qquad \partial:\Psi^0(A'')\longrightarrow\Psi^1(A'),\]

for every left exact cofibered category \(\Psi\) over \(C\), by associating with an element \(u\) of the first member the class in the second member of the element of \(\Psi(A')\), determined up to canonical isomorphism, whose image in \(\Psi([A\longrightarrow A''])\) (notations of 3.3.2) is the pair \((X_0,u)\), where \(X_0\) is the trivial object of \(\Psi(A)\). This being so, I claim that in the case which interests us \(\Psi=\Psi_{L_\bullet}\), the homomorphism (3.3.6.2) defined by the "geometric" route is none other than the boundary homomorphism \(\partial\) of (3.3.6.1) with the sign changed, hence that this last exact sequence is none other (with the identifications made and changing the sign of the \(\partial\)) than that of (loc. cit. (2.5.2)). This is in fact contained in the last assertion of (loc. cit. 5.4), taking into account the compatibilities pointed out in 3.3.5.

Remark 3.3.6.3. Let us take this opportunity to clarify an unclear point of [4, n° 3.11], where the fundamental isomorphisms (3.11.3) and (3.11.4) are defined (which intervene in an essential way in the proof of (loc. cit. 5.4)). The diagram of loc. cit. p.35 is commutative, and not anticommutative, hence does not without further ado define a homomorphism \(u:[L^X_1\longrightarrow L^X_0]\longrightarrow K^\bullet[1]\). It should be made anticommutative by replacing \(u^1\) there by \(-u^1\). (It would have been possible to follow the opposite convention, replacing \(u^0\) by \(-u^0\); but it is the convention indicated (\(u^1\) becoming \(-u^1\)) which gives an isomorphism (3.11.3) generalizing the isomorphism (3.5.3) of loc. cit., as announced in loc. cit.). This definition being thus made precise, the assertion made in loc. cit. 5.4 should be corrected: the two coboundary homomorphisms considered there differ by the sign.

3.3.7. In (loc. cit. 6.13) one has also made precise the variance of the cofibered category \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}\) over \(C\), for variable \(L_\bullet\), by reconstructing in particular the category of cocartesian morphisms of \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}\) into \(\Psi_{L'_\bullet}\) in terms of the chain complexes \(L_\bullet\) and \(L'_\bullet\). One finds in particular that the set of classes up to isomorphism of cocartesian functors \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}\longrightarrow \Psi_{L'_\bullet}\) is in canonical one-to-one correspondence with the set of isomorphisms \(\operatorname{Hom}_{D(C)}(L'_\bullet,L_\bullet)\). We shall use only a small part of this information, which we shall develop in the section that follows, using the simple description of \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}\) given in 3.1.
3.4. Variance of \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}\) with \(L_\bullet\).
Consider then a morphism of complexes
\[(3.4.1)\qquad f:L'_\bullet\longrightarrow L_\bullet,\]
given by a commutative diagram

\[(3.4.2)\qquad \begin{array}{ccccccc} \longrightarrow&L'_2&\longrightarrow&L'_1&\longrightarrow&L'_0&\longrightarrow0\\ &\downarrow f_2&&\downarrow f_1&&\downarrow f_0&\\ \longrightarrow&L_2&\longrightarrow&L_1&\longrightarrow&L_0&\longrightarrow0. \end{array}\]
We shall associate with it a canonical functor
\[(3.4.3)\qquad f^*:\Psi_{L_\bullet}\longrightarrow\Psi_{L'_\bullet},\]

which will in fact be a cocartesian functor of cofibered categories over \(C\) (i.e. it is compatible with the structural functors with values in \(C\), and transforms cocartesian morphisms into cocartesian morphisms, cf. SGA 1 VI). If \((X,\alpha)\) is an object of \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}\), we denote by \(f^*(X,\alpha)\) the object \((X',\alpha')\), where \(X'\) is the extension of \(L'_0\) by \(A\) inverse image of the given extension \(X\) of \(L_0\) by \(A\) by means of \(f_0\), and where \(\alpha'\) is the partial trivialization deduced from the partial trivialization \(\alpha\) by means of the transitivity in the second square of (3.4.2). (The compatibility condition of \(\alpha'\) with the relation \(d'_0d'_1=0\) follows evidently from the analogous condition for \(\alpha\).) It is trivial by construction that the functor (3.4.3) commutes with the structural functors with values in \(C\). The fact that it is cocartesian follows at once from the fact that in the category of extensions of objects of \(C\), the operations "direct image of extensions" commute with the operations "inverse image of extensions".
Note that the functor (3.4.3) defines homomorphisms of functors
\[(3.4.4)\qquad f^i:\Psi^i_{L_\bullet}\longrightarrow\Psi^i_{L'_\bullet},\qquad i=0,1\]
(compare 2.6). On the other hand, the homomorphism (3.4.1) defines homomorphisms
\[(3.4.5)\qquad f^i:\operatorname{Ext}^i(L_\bullet,-)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Ext}^i(L'_\bullet,-),\qquad i\in\mathbb Z.\]

This being so, I claim that for \(i=0,1\), the homomorphisms (3.4.4) and (3.4.5) are compatible with the isomorphisms of the form (3.1.8) and (3.2.4), i.e. that we have commutativity in the following diagrams (\(A\in\operatorname{ob}C\), \(i=0,1\)):

\[(3.4.6)\qquad\xymatrix@C=5em@R=3em{ {\Psi^i_{L_\bullet}(A)}\ar[r]^{\sim}\ar[d]&{\operatorname{Ext}^i(L_\bullet,A)}\ar[d]\\ {\Psi^i_{L'_\bullet}(A)}\ar[r]^{\sim}&{\operatorname{Ext}^i(L'_\bullet,A)}}.\]
The verification of this compatibility is essentially trivial, and left to the reader.

blocks.title: Proposition 3.4.7.
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.4.7.

For the functor (3.4.3) to be faithful (resp. fully faithful, resp. an equivalence of categories), it is necessary and sufficient that \(H_0(f):H_0(L'_\bullet)\longrightarrow H_0(L_\bullet)\) be an epimorphism (resp. that \(H_0(f)\) be an isomorphism and \(H_1(f)\) be an epimorphism, resp. that \(H_0(f)\) and \(H_1(f)\) be isomorphisms).

Indeed, one must express that, \(f^i\) being the functorial homomorphism of (3.4.4) (\(i=0,1\)), \(f^0\) is a monomorphism (resp. \(f^0\) is an isomorphism and \(f^1\) a monomorphism, resp. \(f^0\) and \(f^1\) are isomorphisms). By virtue of the commutativity of (3.4.6), these conditions can be expressed in terms of the analogous conditions on the functors \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\), \(i=0,1\). In this case, the assertion is only a rather trivial statement of homological algebra, which we leave to the reader as an exercise.
3.4.8. Consider now an exact sequence of chain complexes
\[(3.4.8.1)\qquad0\longrightarrow L_\bullet\xrightarrow f L'_\bullet \xrightarrow g L''_\bullet\longrightarrow0,\]
whence functors of the type (3.4.3)
\[\Psi_{L''_\bullet}\xrightarrow{g^*}\Psi_{L'_\bullet} \xrightarrow{f^*}\Psi_{L_\bullet}.\]
The transitivity property one guesses for the functors of type (3.4.3) furnishes here an isomorphism of functors
\[f^*g^*\xrightarrow{\sim}(gf)^*=(0_{L_\bullet\to L''_\bullet})^*,\]

and as the last member is evidently canonically isomorphic to the "null" cocartesian functor from \(\Psi_{L''_\bullet}\) into \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}\), the preceding isomorphism makes it possible to define a canonical functor
\[(3.4.8.2)\qquad\Psi_{L''_\bullet}\longrightarrow \Psi_{[L_\bullet\longrightarrow L'_\bullet]},\]

where \(\Psi_{[L_\bullet\longrightarrow L'_\bullet]}\) denotes the category of pairs \((X',t)\), where \(X'\) is an object of \(\Psi_{L'_\bullet}\) and \(t\) a "trivialization" of its inverse image \(f^*(X')\) i.e. an isomorphism \(f^*(X')\xrightarrow{\sim}0_{L_\bullet,A}\), \(0_{L_\bullet,A}\) denoting the null object of \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}(A)\), and \(A\) being the object of \(C\) under \(X'\). This being so, I claim that the functor (3.4.8.2) is an equivalence of categories (which completes, by a property of exactness in \(L_\bullet\) of the expression \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}(A)\), the property of left exactness in \(A\) established in 3.3.1).

Let us sketch the proof of the preceding assertion. The datum of an object of the second member of (3.4.8.2) amounts to the datum of an extension \(0\longrightarrow A\xrightarrow{i'}X'\xrightarrow{j'}L'_0\longrightarrow0\) of \(L'_0\) by an object \(A\) of \(C\), plus a morphism \(\alpha':L'_1\longrightarrow X'\) satisfying \(j'\alpha'=d'_0\) and \(\alpha'd'_1=0\), plus a morphism \(t:L_0\longrightarrow X'\) satisfying \(j't=f_0\) and \(td_0=\alpha'f_1\), cf. diagram below:

\[\xymatrix@C=2.4em@R=2.2em{ &&0&&0\\ &&A\ar[u]\ar[d]^{i'}&&A\ar[u]\ar[d]^{i''}\\ &L_0\ar[ru]^t&&X'\ar[lu]_{j'}\ar[ru]&&X''\ar[lu]_{j''}\\ 0\ar[r]&L_0\ar[r]^{f_0}\ar[d]_{d_0}&L'_0\ar[r]^{g_0}\ar[d]^{d'_0}&L''_0\ar[r]\ar[d]^{d''_0}&0\\ 0\ar[r]&L_1\ar[r]^{f_1}\ar[d]&L'_1\ar[r]^{g_1}\ar[d]^{d'_1}\ar[u]_{\alpha'}&L''_1\ar[r]\ar[d]^{d''_1}\ar[u]_{\alpha''}&0\\ 0\ar[r]&L_2\ar[r]&L'_2\ar[r]&L''_2\ar[r]&0 }\qquad \begin{cases}j'\alpha'=d'_0,\\ \alpha'd'_1=0,\\ j't=f_0,\\ td_0=\alpha'f_1. \end{cases}\]

A well-known property of left exactness in the first argument of the extensions of an object \(L\) by another \(A\), applied to the first row of the diagram, shows that the datum of \(t\) satisfying \(j't=f_0\) makes it possible to interpret the extension \(X'\) as the inverse image of an extension \(X''\) of \(L''_0\) by \(A\), in such a way that \(t\) corresponds to the natural trivialization of the inverse image of this extension by \(g_0f_0=0\). The datum of \(\alpha'\) satisfying the relations \(j'\alpha'=d'_0\) and \(\alpha'f_1=td_0\) can be interpreted by essentially the same dictionary, but applied to the second row of the diagram, as coming from a morphism \(\alpha'':L''_1\longrightarrow X''\) satisfying \(j''\alpha''=d''_0\) (interpreting \(\alpha'\) and \(\alpha''\) as trivializations of the inverse images of the extensions \(X'\) resp. \(X''\) by \(d'_0\) resp. \(d''_0\)). Finally the relation \(\alpha'd'_1=0\) is then equivalent to the relation \(\alpha''d''_1=0\). In short, the datum of \((X',\alpha',t)\), an object of \(\Psi_{[L_\bullet\longrightarrow L'_\bullet]}\), is equivalent to that of \((X'',\alpha'')\), an object of \(\Psi_{L''_\bullet}\), which proves (essentially) the assertion that (3.4.8.2) is indeed an equivalence of categories.
3.4.9. Let us still consider the exact sequence (3.4.8.1), and consider the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) which it defines:

\[(3.4.9.1)\quad0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}^0(L''_\bullet,A) \xrightarrow{g^0}\operatorname{Ext}^0(L'_\bullet,A) \xrightarrow{f^0}\operatorname{Ext}^0(L_\bullet,A) \xrightarrow{\partial^0}\operatorname{Ext}^1(L''_\bullet,A) \xrightarrow{g^1}\operatorname{Ext}^1(L'_\bullet,A) \xrightarrow{f^1}\operatorname{Ext}^1(L_\bullet,A)\longrightarrow\cdots.\]

The canonical isomorphisms of the type (3.1.8) and (3.2.4) allow us to interpret "geometrically" the first six terms of this exact sequence in terms of the categories \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}\), \(\Psi_{L'_\bullet}\) and \(\Psi_{L''_\bullet}\), and the compatibility (3.4.6) allows us likewise to interpret geometrically the arrows \(f^0,g^0,f^1,g^1\) of this diagram. We shall likewise give a geometric interpretation of the arrow \(\partial^0\) of this diagram, using the result just proved, namely that (3.4.8.2) is an equivalence of categories. Indeed, let
\[u\in\operatorname{Ext}^0(L_\bullet,A),\qquad\text{i.e. }u:L_0\longrightarrow A, \quad ud_0=0.\]

Consider the object \((0,\bar u)\) of \(\Psi_{[L_\bullet\longrightarrow L'_\bullet]}\), where \(0\) is the null object of \(\Psi_{L'_\bullet}\), whose inverse image by \(f_0\) is thus identified with the null object of \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}\), and where \(\bar u\) is the automorphism of this last object defined by \(u\), thanks to the canonical isomorphism (3.1.8). Thanks to the fact that (3.4.8.2) is an equivalence, the preceding object \((0,\bar u)\) comes from an object of \(\Psi_{L''_\bullet}(A)\), defined up to unique isomorphism. The class up to isomorphism of this element is an element of \(\Psi^1_{L''_\bullet}(A)\simeq \operatorname{Ext}^1(L''_\bullet,A)\). I claim that this element is none other than \(\partial^0(u)\).

We shall prove this compatibility by reducing it to the analogous compatibility, stated in [4, cor. 5.5] (where the sign should be corrected as indicated in 3.3.6.3); our argument will give at the same time another proof of the equivalence (3.4.8.2), by reducing it to the analogous statement [4, prop. 2.7]. For this, consider the opposite category \(C^o\) of \(C\), and let us interpret the constructions of \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}(A)\), for fixed \(A\), and those of 3.4.8 and of the present section 3.4.9, in terms of the category \(C^o\), and of the cofibered category \(E\) over \(C^o\) of extensions of the fixed object \(A\) of \(C^o\) by a variable object \(L\) of \(C^o\). It is an additive cofibered category over \(C^o\), and even left exact over \(C^o\) (in the sense of loc. cit. 2.2). A chain complex \(L_\bullet\) of \(C\) can be interpreted as a complex of cochains of \(C^o\), and with this interpretation, the construction given of \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}(A)\) coincides with that of the category opposite to \(\widehat E(L_\bullet)\) in the sense of loc. cit. 1.8. The functor (3.4.8.2) is then identified with the opposite of the functor of the type of loc. cit. (2.1.2), where \(E\) is replaced by its extension \(\widehat E\) to the category of cochain complexes, and the fact that the functor under consideration is an equivalence is thus a special case of loc. cit. 2.7 a) \(\Longrightarrow\) d). On the other hand, the exact sequence (3.4.9.1) is identified with the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Ext}^i(A,L'')\), \(\operatorname{Ext}^i(A,L')\) and \(\operatorname{Ext}^i(A,L)\), modulo a sign \((-1)^i\) for the \(\partial^i\) (Verdier dixit). Similarly, the isomorphisms (3.1.8) and (3.2.4) are identified with the analogous isomorphisms (3.11.5) and (3.11.4). Hence the stated compatibility is a special case of loc. cit. 5.5 (corrected as indicated above).

Remark 3.4.9.2. The writer, who is very much afraid of signs, confesses that he does not entirely trust the preceding proof, fearing that he may have forgotten some signs in the passage from \(C\) to \(C^o\). He recommends that the courageous reader make a direct verification of the announced compatibility (resp. of the opposite compatibility). Let us note that these questions of signs will (fortunately) play no role in the following exposés.
3.5. A canonical flat partial resolution for an arbitrary \(\mathbb Z\)-Module

We shall apply the preceding results to the case where \(C\) is the category of commutative Groups of the topos \(T\) i.e. of \(\mathbb Z\)-Modules of \(T\). Let \(P\) be an object of \(C\), i.e. a commutative group of the topos \(T\). We shall define a chain complex \(L_\bullet(P)\) augmented toward \(P\), depending functorially on \(P\):

\[(3.5.1)\qquad 0\longrightarrow \underbrace{\mathbb Z[P\times P]\times\mathbb Z[P\times P\times P]}_{L_2} \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{d_1}} \underbrace{\mathbb Z[P\times P]}_{L_1} \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{d_0}} \underbrace{\mathbb Z[P]}_{L_0}\longrightarrow0, \qquad \mathbb Z[P]\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\epsilon}}P\]

satisfying \(L_i(P)=0\) for \(i\geq3\). The components of this complex are made explicit in (3.5.1) above, using the notations of 1.4. To make explicit the differential operators \(d_0,d_1\) and the augmentation \(\epsilon\), we use the notation \([p]\) to denote the point of \(\mathbb Z[P](S)\) defined by the point \(p\in P(S)\) of \(P\), and similarly for the notations \([p,q]\), \([p,q,r]\), when \(p,q,r\in P(S)\), to denote elements of \(\mathbb Z[P\times P](S)\) resp. of \(\mathbb Z[P\times P\times P](S)\). With these notations, we shall define \(\epsilon\), \(d_0\) and \(d_1\) by the formulas
\[(3.5.2)\qquad \begin{cases} \epsilon[p]=p,\\ d_0[p,q]=[p+q]-[p]-[q],\\ d_1[p,q]=[p,q]-[q,p],\\ d_1[p,q,r]=[p+q,r]-[p,q+r]+[p,q]-[q,r]. \end{cases}\]
One verifies easily that the homomorphisms of commutative Groups thus defined do indeed define a complex \(L_\bullet(P)\) augmented toward \(P\), i.e. that we have the relations
\[\epsilon d_0=0,\qquad d_1d_0=0.\]

In fact, the first relation is trivial, and the second decomposes into two, according to the two factors of \(L_2(P)\), the two relations in question expressing respectively the commutativity and the associativity of the composition law given on \(P\).
It is immediate that the augmented complex thus defined depends functorially on \(P\), a morphism \(u:P\longrightarrow P'\) giving rise to a commutative diagram
\[\begin{CD} L_\bullet(P) @>{L_\bullet(u)}>> L_\bullet(P')\\ @VVV @VVV\\ P @>{u}>> P'. \end{CD}\]
Let us note also that the components of the complex \(L_\bullet\) are flat, like every free \(\mathbb Z\)-Module.

Proposition 3.5.3.

We have \(H_1(L_\bullet(P))=0\), and the augmentation \(\epsilon\) induces an isomorphism \(H_0(L_\bullet(P))\simeq P\).

To prove 3.5.3, one can reduce by the standard procedure to the case where \(T\) is the category of sets, and where \(P\) is thus an ordinary \(\mathbb Z\)-Module, where (3.5.1) has a chance of being "well known". We shall give a direct proof, not using this reduction, by applying 3.4.7 to the augmentation morphism
\[\epsilon:L_\bullet(P)\longrightarrow P,\]
where \(P\) is as usual considered as a chain complex reduced to dimension zero. We are thus reduced to proving that for every object \(G\) of \(C\), the functor
\[(*)\qquad \epsilon^*:\Psi_P(G)\longrightarrow\Psi_{L_\bullet(P)}(G)\]

is an equivalence of categories. Now the first member of (*) is evidently isomorphic to the category of extensions of \(\mathbb Z\)-Modules of \(P\) by \(G\). To make explicit the second member, we interpret the categories of extensions of \(L_0\), \(L_1\), \(L_2\) by \(G\), as the category of torsors under \(G_P\), resp. the category of torsors under \(G_{P\times P}\), resp. the category of pairs of a torsor under \(G_{P\times P}\) and a torsor under \(G_{P\times P\times P}\), thanks to 1.4. Hence an object of the second member of (*) is identified with a pair \((E,\varphi)\), where \(E\) is a torsor under \(G_P\) and \(\varphi\) a trivialization of the torsor \(E'\) on \(P\times P\) which corresponds to the inverse image by \(d_0\) of the extension of \(\mathbb Z[P]\) by \(G\) defined by \(E\), — \(\varphi\) being subject to a compatibility condition with \(d_0d_1=0\) to be made precise. Now the definition of \(d_0\) (3.5.2) gives at once
\[E'=\pi^*(E)\mathbin{\operatorname{pr}_1^*(E)^{-1}} \mathbin{\operatorname{pr}_2^*(E)^{-1}},\]
and a trivialization of this torsor can be interpreted as an isomorphism (also denoted \(\varphi\))
\[\pi^*(E)\mathrel{\mathop{\simeq}^{\varphi}} \operatorname{pr}_1^*(E)\operatorname{pr}_2^*(E).\]

On the other hand, in making explicit the compatibility condition of \(\varphi\) with \(d_1d_0=0\), one finds that it is expressed by the commutativity condition of (1.2.1), and by the associativity condition of (1.1.4.1), these two conditions corresponding respectively to the two factors of \(L_2\). The fact that (*) is an equivalence of categories is then none other than the dictionary of 1.1.6. This completes the proof of 3.5.3.

Remark 3.5.4. a) It would be interesting to strengthen 3.5.3 by the construction of a resolution \(L_\bullet(P)\) of \(P\), depending functorially on \(P\), and whose components would be isomorphic (functorially in \(P\)) to sums of free \(\mathbb Z\)-modules generated by cartesian powers of \(P\). Taking into account the isomorphisms of the form (1.4.5), this would indeed give a method of partial computation of the \(\operatorname{Ext}^i(P,A)\) as the abutment of a spectral sequence whose initial term would be made explicit in terms of the \(H^j(P\times P\ldots\times P,A)\). (Here we find only very partial information on the \(\operatorname{Ext}^1\), to the exclusion of the higher \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\)). There is in any case a natural way to continue the partial resolution \(L_\bullet(P)\) one step further.(*)

b) One can give a variant of the construction (3.5.1) and of 3.5.3 in the category of \(A\)-Modules, where \(A\) is a fixed Ring of \(T\). The form of the complex \(L_\bullet(P)\) becomes more complicated, because of the necessity of complicating 1.1.6 (cf. 2.10.3). One finds
\[(3.5.5)\qquad \begin{cases} L_0=A[P],\\ L_1=A[P\times P]+A[A\times P],\\ L_2=A[P\times P\times P]+A[P\times P]+A[A\times A\times P]+A[A\times P\times P], \end{cases}\]
the structure of complex augmented toward \(P\) being given by the formulas (3.5.2), completed by the formulas

\[(3.5.6)\qquad \begin{cases} d_0[\lambda,p]=[\lambda p]-\lambda[p],\\ d_1[\lambda,\mu,p]=[\lambda,\mu p]-[\lambda\mu,p]+\lambda[\mu,p],\\ d_1[\lambda,p,q]=[\lambda,p+q]-[\lambda,p]-[\lambda,q]+\lambda[p,q]-[\lambda p,\lambda q]. \end{cases}\]

The relation \(d_0d_1=0\) follows from the analogous relation for the restriction of the operators \(d_0,d_1\) to the components considered in (3.5.2) (which, as we have seen, was equivalent to the associativity and the commutativity of the addition law given in \(P\)), and from the relations \(d_0d_1(\lambda,\mu,p)=0\) and \(d_0d_1(\lambda,p,q)=0\), which, as one verifies immediately, are equivalent to defining more generally the notion of biextension of \(A\)-Modules of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), which is a variant of that of extension of \(A\)-Modules. Such a biextension is defined as a biextension in the sense of 2.1, but with the underlying partial extension structures being enriched into structures of extensions of \(A_Q\)-Modules resp. of \(A_P\)-Modules (compatible with the structures of relative Modules that we already have on \(Q_P,G_P\) and \(P_Q,G_Q\)). The two structures of relative Modules on \(E\) can be interpreted in terms of two operations of the multiplicative monoid underlying \(A\) on the sheaf of sets underlying \(E\), and one assumes that these two actions commute with one another; this compatibility is thus added to the one expressed by the commutativity of the diagram (2.1.1), and is expressed by the commutativity of an analogous diagram, which we leave to the reader the task of writing. Finally, one assumes that the first operation of \(A\) on \(E\) is distributive, not only with respect to the first composition law of \(E\), but also for the second composition law of \(E\); and similarly for the second operation of \(A\) on \(E\).

One will observe that the dictionary made explicit in 1.1.6 also admits an \(A\)-linear variant, which can be expressed by saying that the datum of an extension of the \(A\)-Module \(P\) by another \(G\) amounts to the datum of a family of torsors \(E_p\) under the \(G_S\), parametrized by the "points" \(p\in P(S)\) (\(S\in\operatorname{ob}T\) being a variable object), \(E_p\) being required to vary in an \(A\)-linear way (and not only additively as in 1.1.6) in \(p\). This is made explicit by the datum, in addition to the isomorphisms \(\varphi_{p,p'}\) (1.1.3.1), of the datum of isomorphisms
\[\mu_{a,p}:a^*(P_p)\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}}P_{ap} \qquad(p\in P(S),\ a\in A(S)),\]

where the first member denotes the image of the \(G_S\)-torsor \(P_p\) by the extension of scalars \(g\longmapsto ag:G_S\longrightarrow G_S\). We leave to the reader the task of making explicit the conditions to be imposed on these data \(\varphi,\mu\) so that they indeed correspond to an extension of Modules \(E\) of \(P\) by \(G\) (cf. 3.5.4 b)).
3.6. Application to the description of the cofibered category of biextensions of a given pair \((P,Q)\) of commutative Groups

Let \(P,Q\) be two \(\mathbb Z\)-Modules of the topos \(T\). Let us choose arbitrarily a flat resolution \(L'_\bullet(P)\) of \(P\), coinciding in dimension \(\leq2\) with the augmented complex \(L_\bullet(P)\) of 3.5, — which is possible thanks to 3.5.3 and thanks to the fact that every \(\mathbb Z\)-Module over \(T\) is a quotient of a free \(\mathbb Z\)-Module, hence a flat one. Let us likewise choose a flat resolution \(L'_\bullet(Q)\) of \(Q\) coinciding with \(L_\bullet(Q)\) in dimensions \(\leq2\). We thus have canonical homomorphisms
\[(3.6.1)\qquad L_\bullet(P)\longrightarrow L'_\bullet(P),\qquad L_\bullet(Q)\longrightarrow L'_\bullet(Q),\]
inducing a canonical homomorphism

\[(3.6.2)\qquad L_\bullet(P,Q)\mathrel{\mathop=^{\rm dfn}} L_\bullet(P)\otimes L_\bullet(Q)\longrightarrow L'_\bullet(P,Q)\mathrel{\mathop=^{\rm dfn}}L'_\bullet(P)\otimes L'_\bullet(Q) \mathrel{\mathop\simeq}P\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^{L}}Q,\]
which is an isomorphism on the components of dimension \(\leq2\). Consequently we have, by virtue of the definitions,
\[(3.6.3)\qquad \Psi_{L_\bullet(P,Q)}\simeq\Psi_{L'_\bullet(P,Q)}.\]

Theorem 3.6.4. The additive cofibered category, over the category \(C=\operatorname{GRAB}(T)\) of commutative Groups of \(T\), formed of the biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by a variable abelian Group \(G\) (2.4), is canonically equivalent to the additive cofibered category \(\Psi_{L_\bullet(P,Q)}=\Psi_{L'_\bullet(P,Q)}\), defined in terms of \(L_\bullet(P,Q)=L_\bullet(P)\otimes L_\bullet(Q)\) as in 3.1.
From this, and from 3.2.5, one deduces the principal result of the present section, announced above:
Corollary 3.6.5. For fixed \(P\) and \(Q\), we have a canonical isomorphism, functorial in \(G\):
\[(3.6.5.1)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\mathrel{\mathop\simeq} \operatorname{Ext}^1(P\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^{L}}Q,G).\]
Let us prove 3.6.5, and for this let us first make explicit the components of dimension 0, 1 and 2 of \(L_\bullet(P,Q)\):

\[(3.6.6)\qquad \begin{cases} L_0(P,Q)=L_0(P)\otimes L_0(Q)=\mathbb Z[P\times Q],\\ L_1(P,Q)=L_0(P)\otimes L_1(Q)+L_1(P)\otimes L_0(Q) =\mathbb Z[P\times Q\times Q]+\mathbb Z[P\times P\times Q],\\ L_2(P,Q)=L_0(P)\otimes L_2(Q)+L_1(P)\otimes L_1(Q)+L_2(P)\otimes L_0(Q)\\ \hspace{4.8em}=\mathbb Z[P\times Q\times Q]+\mathbb Z[P\times Q\times Q\times Q] +\mathbb Z[P\times P\times Q\times Q]\\ \hspace{6.2em}+\mathbb Z[P\times P\times Q]+\mathbb Z[P\times P\times P\times Q]. \end{cases}\]

To make explicit the cofibered category \(\Psi_{L_\bullet(P,Q)}\) via its fiber categories \(\Psi_{L_\bullet(P,Q)}(G)\), we use 1.4 to make explicit the extensions of the \(L_i(P,Q)\) (for \(i=0,1,2\)) by \(G\), in terms of torsors. Thus, an extension of \(L_0(P,Q)\) by \(G\) is identified with a torsor under \(G_{P\times Q}\), an extension of \(L_1(P,Q)\) by \(G\) is identified with a pair of a torsor under \(G_{P\times Q\times Q}\) and another under \(G_{P\times P\times Q}\), and similarly an extension of \(L_2(P,Q)\) by \(G\) is identified with a system of five torsors, under the groups deduced from \(G\) by base change over the bases \(P\times Q\times Q,P\times Q\times Q\times Q,P\times P\times Q\times Q,P\times P\times Q,P\times P\times P\times Q\) respectively. Using this dictionary, one can interpret a pair \((X,\alpha)\) of an extension of \(L_0(P,Q)\) by \(G\), and of a trivialization of \(d_0^*(X)\) over \(L_1(P,Q)\), as a torsor \(E\) over \(P\times Q\), equipped with a pair \((\varphi,\psi)\) of trivializations of the pair of torsors \((E_1,E_2)\) over \(P\times Q\times Q\) resp. \(P\times P\times Q\), deduced from \(E\) by inverse image by means of \(d_0:L_1(P,Q)\longrightarrow L_0(P,Q)\). Making explicit the differential operator \(d_0\), one finds as in the proof of 3.5.3 that \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) can be interpreted as isomorphisms
\[\varphi:\pi_Q^*(E)\mathrel{\mathop{\longleftarrow}^{\sim}}q_1^*(E)q_2^*(E), \qquad \psi:\pi_P^*(E)\mathrel{\mathop{\longleftarrow}^{\sim}}p_1^*(E)p_2^*(E),\]

where \(\pi_Q,q_1,q_2\) are the three morphisms \(P\times Q\times Q\longrightarrow Q\) deduced respectively from the three morphisms \(Q\times Q\longrightarrow Q\) composition law, first and second projection, and where \(\pi_P,p_1,p_2:P\times P\times Q\longrightarrow P\times Q\) are defined symmetrically. Hence \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) can be interpreted as data of the type (2.0.4) and (2.0.7) which figure in 2.1. Finally, the condition that the pair \((X,\alpha)\) indeed be an object of \(\Psi_{L_\bullet(P,Q)}(G)\), i.e. that the partial trivialization \(\alpha\) be compatible with the relation \(d_0d_1=0\), is expressed in terms of the structure \((E,\varphi,\psi)\) by five compatibility relations, in terms of torsors over \(P\times Q\times Q,P\times Q\times Q\times Q,P\times P\times Q\times Q,P\times P\times Q,P\times P\times P\times Q\) respectively, these five relations corresponding to the five terms occurring in the expression (3.6.6) of \(L_2(P,Q)\). One then verifies as in the proof of 3.5.3 that the first two relations are none other than the relations (2.0.6) of commutativity and (2.0.5) of associativity, whose conjunction expresses that \(\varphi\) defines on \(E\) a structure of commutative extension of \(Q_P\) by \(G_P\). Symmetrically, the last two conditions express that \(\psi\) defines on \(E\) a structure of commutative extension of \(P_Q\) by \(G_Q\). Finally, the middle condition, bearing on torsors over \(P\times P\times Q\times Q\), is none other than the commutativity of the diagram (2.1.1), which expresses that \(\varphi\) and \(\psi\) are compatible, i.e. (taking the other conditions into account) that they define on \(E\) a structure of biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\). The verification of these interpretations is essentially trivial, and left to the reader. The reader will doubtless grant us that the preceding discussions indeed define an equivalence of cofibered categories over \(C=\operatorname{GRAB}(T)\), which is the one announced in 3.6.4.

Remark 3.6.7. Each of the three generalizations pointed out in 2.10 of the notion of biextension makes it possible to give a corresponding variant of 3.6.4 (hence also of 3.6.5). The generalization to the case of an arbitrary number of factors (cf. 2.10.2) is trivial, and the generalization to the case of biextensions of \(A\)-Modules (cf. 2.10.3) is immediate, once one has obtained the variant of the construction of \(L_\bullet(P)\) in that case, pointed out in 3.5.4 b). More interesting from the point of view of homological algebra would seem to be the generalization to the case where \(P\) and \(Q\) are not assumed commutative (cf. 2.10.1). It is then appropriate to associate with a Group \(P\) (not necessarily commutative) of \(T\) a complex truncated at order 2, say \(K_\bullet(P)\), which is described in a way analogous to \(L_\bullet(P)\) in 3.5, with the difference that one takes \(K_2(P)=\mathbb Z[P\times P\times P]\) (thus omitting the first factor which figured in \(L_2(P)\)), \(d_0\) and \(d_1\) being defined as in (3.5.2). Here \(H_0(K_\bullet(P))\) is identified with the Group \(P/[P,P]\) deduced from \(P\) by making it abelian, which one can also identify with the sheaf \(H_1(P,\mathbb Z)\) of homology groups of degree 1 of \(P\) with coefficients in \(\mathbb Z\), and \(H_1(K_\bullet(P))\) is likewise isomorphic to the sheaf \(H_2(P,\mathbb Z)\); in fact, \(K_\bullet(P)\) is none other than the truncation at order 2 of the complex of chains of \(P\) with coefficients in \(\mathbb Z\), namely \(C_\bullet(P,\mathbb Z)\). This said, the argument establishing 3.5.3 establishes likewise that the cofibered category \(\Psi_{K_\bullet(P)}\) over \(C=\operatorname{GRAB}(T)\) is canonically equivalent to the category of central extensions of \(P\) by variable commutative Groups \(G\). One finds likewise, for two Groups \(P,Q\), an equivalence of the cofibered category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) in the sense of 2.10.1, with the cofibered category \(\Psi_{K_\bullet(P,Q)}\), where
\[K_\bullet(P,Q)=K_\bullet(P)\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^{L}}K_\bullet(Q).\]

These reflections suggest that one will perhaps find, for \(P\) commutative, a canonical resolution of \(P\) as desired in 3.5.4 a), by a judicious modification of the chain complex \(C_\bullet(P,\mathbb Z)\).
3.7. Various compatibilities

In the present section, we examine the behavior of the equivalence of cofibered categories 3.6.4, for variable \(P\) and \(Q\). The variance of the cofibered category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by a variable \(G\), with respect to variable \(P,Q\), is defined in principle in 2.4, and can also be formulated by the assertion that we have a cofibering functor (2.6.1). One will note that the variance in \(P,Q\) is contravariant. The variance of the cofibered category \(\Psi_{L_\bullet(P,Q)}\) with respect to variable \(P,Q\) is likewise clear, since \(L_\bullet(P,Q)=L_\bullet(P)\otimes L_\bullet(Q)\) depends functorially on the pair \((P,Q)\), and \(\Psi_{L_\bullet}\) depends contravariantly on \(L_\bullet\) (3.4). This being so, a first compatibility is expressed, for every triple of morphisms
\[(3.7.1)\qquad P'\longrightarrow P,\qquad Q'\longrightarrow Q,\qquad G\longrightarrow G',\]
by the fact that the diagram of functors
\[(3.7.2)\qquad \begin{CD} \Psi_{L_\bullet(P,Q)}(G)@>>>\Psi_{L_\bullet(P',Q')}(G')\\ @VVV@VVV\\ \operatorname{BIEXT}(P,Q;G)@>>>\operatorname{BIEXT}(P',Q';G') \end{CD}\]

is commutative up to a canonical isomorphism. We shall admit this compatibility, since the explicitation of the commutativity isomorphism can only be as obvious as it is tedious (just like, for that matter, the complete explicitation which it presupposes of the equivalence of cofibered categories 3.6.4).

3.7.3. From the preceding compatibility it follows in particular that the iso- morphism (3.6.5.1) is functorial not only in \(G\), but also in \(P\) and \(Q\). One can state likewise, and more trivially, an analogous functoriality for the isomorphism defined via (3.1.8) as (3.6.5.1) was via (3.2.4):
\[(3.7.4)\qquad \operatorname{Biext}^0(P,Q;G)\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{\sim}} \operatorname{Ext}^0(P\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^{L}}Q,G)\simeq \operatorname{hom}(P\otimes Q,G).\]

But one will note that we had already obtained such an isomorphism in (2.5.4.1), and noted (2.6) that it was functorial in its three arguments. Now it is immediate to verify that the two isomorphisms (3.7.4) and (2.5.4.1) are equal.

3.7.5. The interest of the isomorphism of (3.6.5) is that it expresses the remarkable group of geometric nature, \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\), in terms of a very simple invariant of homological algebra \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(P\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^{L}}Q,G)\), which fits into the sequence of invariants \(\operatorname{Ext}^i(P\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^{L}}Q,G)\), to which the usual formalism of homological algebra applies. In particular, the sequence of these \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) is a cohomological functor in the sense of [3] (or connected sequence of functors in the termino- logy of [5]) in each of the three arguments \(P,Q,G\). In particular, an exact sequence
\[(3.7.5.1)\qquad 0\longrightarrow G'\longrightarrow G\longrightarrow G''\longrightarrow0\]
(resp.
\[(3.7.5.2)\qquad 0\longrightarrow P'\longrightarrow P\longrightarrow P''\longrightarrow0, \quad\text{or}\quad 0\longrightarrow Q'\longrightarrow Q\longrightarrow Q''\longrightarrow0)\]

gives rise to an infinite exact sequence on the \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\), whose first six terms (involving only \(\operatorname{Ext}^0\) and \(\operatorname{Ext}^1\)) are interpreted in terms of biextensions thanks to 3.6.5 and 3.7.4. We have already pointed out an interpretation of the exact sequence deduced from (3.7.5.1), and in particular of the coboundary operator
\[(3.7.5.3)\qquad \partial:\operatorname{Biext}^0(P,Q;G'')\longrightarrow \operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G'),\]

in geometric terms, within the framework of the notion of left exact cofibered category (3.3.6). One may propose to give an analogous interpretation of the six-term exact sequence associated with one of the short exact sequences (3.7.5.2), say the first, or what amounts to the same thing taking 3.7.3 into account, to give a geometric interpretation of the coboundary homomorphism
\[(3.7.5.4)\qquad \partial:\operatorname{Biext}^0(P',Q;G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Biext}^1(P'',Q;G).\]
We have indeed:

Proposition 3.7.6.

The cofibered category over \(\operatorname{GRAB}(T)^o\times\operatorname{GRAB}(T)^o\times\operatorname{GRAB}(T)\) of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), with \(P,Q,G\) variable (cf. (2.6.1)) is left exact not only in the argument \(G\), but also in each of the arguments \(P\) and \(Q\). This said, the coboundary homomorphism (3.7.5.4) deduced from the exact sequence of the \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) associated with (3.7.5.2) (thanks to the isomorphisms 3.6.5 and 3.7.4) is the coboundary homomorphism of the theory of left exact cofibered categories over \(C=\operatorname{GRAB}(T)^o\) recalled in 3.3.6, applied to the cofibered category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), where \(Q\) and \(G\) are fixed, and \(P\) is variable (subject to a possible sign error in 3.4.9, (cf. 3.4.9.2)).

Let us sketch the proof of 3.7.6. Thanks to 3.6.4 and the com- patibility (3.7.2), we interpret the biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\) as the objects of \(\Psi_{L_\bullet(P,Q)}(G)\). Consider the homomorphism
\[L_\bullet(P,Q)=L_\bullet(P)\otimes L_\bullet(Q)\longrightarrow P\otimes L_\bullet(Q)\]

defined by the augmentation \(L_\bullet(P)\longrightarrow P\); it follows from 3.5.3 and from the flat- ness of \(L_\bullet(Q)\) that this homomorphism induces an isomorphism on the objects \(H_0\) and \(H_1\), hence (3.4.7) an equivalence \(\Psi_{P\otimes L_\bullet(Q)}\longrightarrow\Psi_{L_\bullet(P,Q)}\). This allows us to interpret the biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\) as objects of \(\Psi_{P\otimes L_\bullet(Q)}(G)\). This being so, the exact sequence (3.7.5.2) gives an exact sequence of chain complexes
\[(3.7.6.1)\qquad 0\longrightarrow P'\otimes L_\bullet(Q)\longrightarrow P\otimes L_\bullet(Q)\longrightarrow P''\otimes L_\bullet(Q)\longrightarrow0.\]

The left exactness announced then follows from 3.4.8 applied to this exact sequence of complexes. (The direct verification would moreover be immediate.) On the other hand, the homomorphism (3.7.5.4) is none other than the co- boundary homomorphism \(\operatorname{Ext}^0\longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}^1\) deduced from this exact sequence, by taking the \(\operatorname{Ext}^i\) with values in \(G\), and the last assertion of 3.7.6 is thus a special case of 3.4.9.
3.8. Biextensions of \((P,Q)\) trivialized on extensions of \(P\) and \(Q\)
Let us first consider two exact sequences of abelian Groups

\[(3.8.1)\qquad 0\longrightarrow L_1\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{i}}L_0 \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{u}}P\longrightarrow0,\quad 0\longrightarrow M_1\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{j}}M_0 \mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{v}}Q\longrightarrow0.\]

We propose to describe the category of biextensions \(E\) of \((P,Q)\) by an abelian Group \(G\), such that the inverse image \((u,v)^*(E)\) of \(E\) by \((u,v)\) is trivial, i.e. such that there exists a homomorphism \(s\) making the following triangle commutative
\[(3.8.2)\qquad \begin{CD} &E\\ @A{s}AA @VVV\\ L_0\times M_0@>{u\times v}>>P\times Q \end{CD}\]

and satisfying the requisite additivity condition in each of its two arguments. The datum of such an \(s\) thus makes it possible to identify \((u,v)^*(E)\) with the trivial biextension \(E_0=L_0\times M_0\times G\) of \((L_0,M_0)\) by \(G\). The property of left exactness in \(P,Q\) of the cofibered category of biextensions of \((P,Q)\) by fixed \(G\) (3.7.6) then shows that the datum of the biextension \(E\) of \((P,Q)\), equipped with the trivialization \(s\) of its inverse image, is essentially equivalent to the datum of two partial trivializations of the trivial biex- tension \(E_0\) of \((L_0,M_0)\) by \(G\), on \((L_1,M_0)\) and \((L_0,M_1)\) respective- ly, which coincide on \((L_1,M_1)\). As the biextensions induced by \(E_0\) on \((L_1,M_0),(L_0,M_1)\) and \((L_1,M_1)\) are identified with the trivial biextensions, a partial trivialization on \((L_1,M_0)\) (resp. on \((L_0,M_1)\)) is equivalent to the datum of a homomorphism (or pairing)
\[(3.8.3)\qquad f:L_1\otimes M_0\longrightarrow G \quad(\text{resp. }g:L_0\otimes M_1\longrightarrow G),\]
and the compatibility condition under consideration means simply that the two homomorphisms in question coincide on \(L_1\otimes M_1\), i.e. that we have
\[(3.8.4)\qquad f(x_1\otimes y_1)=g(x_1\otimes y_1)\]
for sections \(x_1,y_1\) of \(L_1,M_1\) on an object of \(T\). In terms of the homo- morphism \(s\) of (3.8.2), the forms \(f\) and \(g\) are characterized by the conditions
which bring out once again the necessity of the condition (3.8.4).
The trivialization \(s\) being chosen, the other trivializations are the morphisms \(s':L_0\times M_0\longrightarrow E\) of the form
with
\[h:L_0\otimes M_0\longrightarrow G\]
an arbitrary form; it is then clear from (3.8.5) that the forms \(f,g\) defined by \(s\) are changed into the forms
\[(3.8.7)\qquad \begin{aligned} f'(x_1\otimes y_0)&=f(x_1\otimes y_0)+h(x_1\otimes y_0)\\ g'(x_0\otimes y_1)&=g(x_0\otimes y_1)+h(x_0\otimes y_1). \end{aligned}\]
One thus finds:

Proposition 3.8.8.

The subgroup of \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\) formed of the classes of biextensions whose inverse image by \((u,v)\) is a trivial biextension of \((L_0,M_0)\) (i.e. the kernel of \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Biext}^1(L_0,M_0;G)\)) is cano- nically isomorphic, thanks to the preceding description, to the quotient group

of the group of pairs of forms \((f,g)\) (3.8.3), satisfying the compatibility condition (3.8.4), modulo the pairs of forms which come by restriction from a form \(h:L_0\otimes M_0\longrightarrow G\).

We now want to interpret this isomorphism in terms of homological algebra, when one takes into account the canonical isomorphism \(\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\simeq\operatorname{Ext}^1(P\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^L}Q,G)\) of 3.6.5. For this, let us interpret the data (3.8.1) as being two complexes which are resolutions of \(P\) resp. of \(Q\),
\[(3.8.9)\qquad L_\bullet:0\longrightarrow L_1\longrightarrow L_0\longrightarrow0, \quad M_\bullet:0\longrightarrow M_1\longrightarrow M_0\longrightarrow0,\]
and let us consider the tensor product complex \(L_\bullet\otimes M_\bullet\).
\[0\longrightarrow L_1\otimes M_1\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{d_2}} L_1\otimes M_0+L_0\otimes M_1\mathrel{\mathop{\longrightarrow}^{d_1}} L_0\otimes M_0\longrightarrow0.\]
One knows moreover that we have a canonical homomorphism in the derived category
\[P\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^L}Q\longrightarrow L_\bullet\otimes M_\bullet,\]
whence a no less canonical homomorphism
\[(3.8.10)\qquad H^1(\operatorname{Hom}^{\bullet}(L_\bullet\otimes M_\bullet,G)) \longrightarrow\operatorname{Ext}^1(P\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^L}Q,G).\]

One will note moreover that the group of 1-cocycles of the complex \(\operatorname{Hom}^{\bullet}(L_\bullet\otimes M_\bullet,G)\) is identified with the group of pairs \((f,g)\) as in (3.8.3), satisfying the compatibility condition (3.8.4), by associating with such a pair the homomorphism \(F:L_1\otimes M_0+L_0\otimes M_1\longrightarrow G\) with components \((f,g)\):
\[(3.8.11)\qquad F(x_1\otimes y_0+x_0\otimes y_1)=f(x_1\otimes y_0)+g(x_0\otimes y_1).\]

Moreover the coboundary of the 0-cochain \(h:L_0\otimes M_0\longrightarrow G\) is none other than the pair of its restrictions to \(L_1\otimes M_0\) and \(L_0\otimes M_1\), so that the group of pairs \((f,g)\) modulo the pairs \((h,h)\) described in 3.8.8 is canonically isomorphic to the first member of (3.8.10). This being so, we have the following compatibility:

Proposition 3.8.12.

If \(\xi\in\operatorname{Ker}(\operatorname{Biext}^1(P,Q;G)\longrightarrow \operatorname{Biext}^1(L_0,M_0;G))\) is described by the pair \((f,g)\) as stated in 3.8.8, then the image of \(\xi\) in \(\operatorname{Ext}^1(P\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^L}Q;G)\) by the isomorphism (3.6.5.1) is the image by (3.8.10) of the element of the first member defined by the cocycle (3.8.11).
The verification of this compatibility is left to the reader.
3.8.13. We shall apply this in particular in the case where one takes
\[L_0=\mathbb Z[P],\qquad M_0=\mathbb Z[Q],\]
\(L_1\) and \(M_1\) being respectively the kernels of the canonical homomorphisms \(\mathbb Z[P]\longrightarrow P\), \(\mathbb Z[Q]\longrightarrow Q\). Let us note that we have canonical isomorphisms
\[L_0\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^L}M_0\simeq L_0\otimes M_0\simeq\mathbb Z[P\times Q],\]
\(L_0\) and \(M_0\) being flat, whence one deduces thanks to (1.4.5) canonical isomorphisms
\[\operatorname{Ext}^i(L_0\mathbin{\mathop\otimes^L}M_0,G)\simeq H^i(P\times Q,G_{P\times Q}).\]

From this and from 3.6.5, or directly thanks to the definitions and to 1.4, one concludes that the functor which associates with every biextension of \((L_0,M_0)\) by \(G\) the torsor under \(G_{P\times Q}\) over \(P\times Q\) which it induces, is an equivalence of categories. (This remains valid without using any additive structure on \(P,Q\).) Consequently, if \(E\) is a biextension of \((P,Q)\) by \(G\), it amounts to the same to give a trivialization of its inverse image on \((L_0,M_0)\), or to give a section of \(E\) considered only as an object over \(P\times Q\), i.e. a trivialization of \(E\) considered as a torsor under \(G_{P\times Q}\).

The biextensions equipped with this supplementary structure are thus described by the pairs \((f,g)\) (3.8.3) satisfying the compatibility (3.8.4), and the description up to isomorphism is the one given in 3.8.8.

Let us note moreover that with the notations of 3.5, one can consider \(L_1\) as the cokernel of \(L_2(P)\longrightarrow L_1(P)\), and similarly for \(M_1\), which makes it possible to make explicit the datum of \((f,g)\) in terms of a system of homomorphisms
\[\begin{cases} f':L_1(P)\otimes L_0(Q)=\mathbb Z[P\times P\times Q]\longrightarrow G,\\ g':L_0(P)\otimes L_1(Q)=\mathbb Z[P\times Q\times Q]\longrightarrow G, \end{cases}\]
i.e. of homomorphisms of sheaves of sets
\[P\times P\times Q\longrightarrow G,\qquad P\times Q\times Q\longrightarrow G,\]

satisfying five compatibility conditions (expressing that \(f'\) and \(g'\) can be factored through \(f\) and \(g\) — which makes \(2\times2\) equations to write, taking into account the form of \(L_2(P)\) and \(L_2(Q)\) as a sum of two terms — and the condition (3.8.4)). One then observes that \(f'\) and \(g'\) are none other than the partial multiplication data defining a biextension, and the five conditions in question are none other than the five axioms of biextensions considered in 2.1 (cf. proof of 3.6.4).
